      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Accounting Standards Codification - Wikipedia",
        "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accounting_Standards_Codification",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "wikipedia.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=wikipedia.org",
          "site_name": "Wikipedia"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "In US accounting practices, the Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) is the current single source of United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). It is maintained by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB). The codification is effective for interim and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. All prior accounting standards documents were superseded as described in FASB Statement No. 168, The FASB Accounting Standards Codification and the Hierarchy of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. Accounting literature not included in the Codification is non-authoritative. The Codification reorganizes the thousands of U.S. GAAP pronouncements into roughly 90 accounting topics and displays all topics using a consistent structure. It also includes relevant U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) guidance that follows the same topical structure in separate sections in the Codification.\n\nThe Financial Accounting Standards Advisory Council then voiced its concerns due to the increase of financial reporting guidance from the old U.S. GAAP standards, and the FASB responded by launching a new project to codify the standards. The project was approved in September 2004 by the Trustees of the Financial Accounting Foundation. All users must register to view any codification information. The codification allows a free basic view or paid professional view to the public. The professional view requires an annual subscription up to $940 depending on concurrent users. Discounts may apply to multiple concurrent users. The codification is used by accounting and reporting professionals, analysts and investors. Each ASC reference is structured as a series of four numbers separated by hyphens: a three-digit Topic (the first digit of which represents an Area), a two-digit Subtopic, a two-digit Section, and a two- or three-digit Paragraph.\n\nIn US accounting practices, the Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) is the current single source of United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). It is maintained by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB). The codification is effective for interim and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. All prior accounting standards documents were superseded as described in FASB Statement No. 168, The FASB Accounting Standards Codification and the Hierarchy of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. Accounting literature not included in the Codification is non-authoritative. The Codification reorganizes the thousands of U.S. GAAP pronouncements into roughly 90 accounting topics and displays all topics using a consistent structure. It also includes relevant U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) guidance that follows the same topical structure in separate sections in the Codification.\n\nThe FASB expected the system to reduce the amount of time and effort required to research accounting issues, mitigate the risk of noncompliance with standards through improved usability of the literature, provide accurate information with real-time updates as new standards are released, and assist the FASB with the research efforts required during the standard-setting process. The three primary goals of the codification are \"simplify user access by codifying all authoritative U.S. GAAP in one spot, ensure that the codification content accurately represented authoritative U.S. GAAP as of July 1, 2009, and to create a codification research system that is up-to-date for the released results of standard-setting activity.\" The codification was made to make accounting standards easier to find through a single database. Before the Codification, accounting standards lacked a consistent and logical structure. For the last 50 years, U.S. GAAP consisted of thousands of standards with multiple sta",
        "is_citable": true,
        "prompt_context_metadata": {
          "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accounting_Standards_Codification",
          "search_provider": "anthropic",
          "age": "October 2, 2025"
        }
      },
      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Financial Accounting Standards Board - Wikipedia",
        "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Financial_Accounting_Standards_Board",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "wikipedia.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=wikipedia.org",
          "site_name": "Wikipedia"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "On July 1, 2009, the FASB announced the launch of its Accounting Standards Codification, an online research system representing the single source of authoritative nongovernmental U.S. GAAP, available from the FASB in multiple views; Professional view, Academic view, and Basic view. The Codification organizes the pronouncements that constitute U.S. GAAP into a consistent, searchable format. The Codification is not to be confused with the FASB&#x27;s 1973 Conceptual Framework project. In 2010, the SEC instructed the staff to create and implement a work plan that addresses whether, when and how U.S. GAAP should be merged into a global reporting model developed by International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)\u2014the standards setting body designated by the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS). The SEC staff research included including convergence with IFRS and an alternate IFRS endorsement mechanism.\n\nThe conceptual framework provides two functions: to state the objectives of financial reporting and provide definitions of financial statement elements. The conceptual framework creates a foundation for financial accounting and establishes consistent standards that highlight the nature, function, and limitations of financial reporting. The FASB formed the Emerging Issues Task Force (EITF) in 1984. It was formed to provide timely responses to financial issues as they emerged. The group includes 15 people from both the private and public sectors coupled with representatives from the FASB and an SEC observer. As issues emerge, the task force considers them and tries to reach a consensus on what course of action to take. From conception until the 2003 AICPA GAAP Agreement, if consensus was reached on a topic, the group would issue an EITF Issue that was considered equivalent to a FASB pronouncement and included in GAAP.\n\nIn 2002, the FASB began to work on a convergence project in partnership with the International Accounting Standards Board (IASB), the independent accounting standard-setting body of the International Financial Reporting Standards Foundation. The two groups met on September 18, 2002, in Norwalk, Connecticut, to sign a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) which \"committed the boards to developing high-quality, compatible accounting standards with a common solution.\" This MoU, which came to be known as the Norwalk Agreement, outlined plans to converge IFRS and U.S. GAAP into one set of high quality and compatible standards. For ten years the FASB and IASB collaborated on a common objective not only to eliminate differences between IFRS and U.S. GAAP wherever possible, \"but also to achieve convergence in accounting standards that stood the test of time.\"\n\nOn July 1, 2009, the FASB announced the launch of its Accounting Standards Codification, an online research system representing the single source of authoritative nongovernmental U.S. GAAP, available from the FASB in multiple views; Professional view, Academic view, and Basic view. The Codification organizes the pronouncements that constitute U.S. GAAP into a consistent, searchable format. The Codification is not to be confused with the FASB's 1973 Conceptual Framework project. In 2010, the SEC instructed the staff to create and implement a work plan that addresses whether, when and how U.S. GAAP should be merged into a global reporting model developed by International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)\u2014the standards setting body designated by the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS). The SEC staff research included including convergence with IFRS and an alternate IFRS endorsement mechanism.\n\nThe Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) is a private standard-setting body whose primary purpose is to establish and improve Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) within the United States in the public's interest. The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) designated the FASB as the organization responsible for setting accounting standards for public companies in the U.S. The FASB replaced the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants' (AICPA) Accounting Principles Board (APB) on July 1, 1973. The FASB is run by the nonprofit Financial Accounting Foundation. FASB accounting standards are accepted as authoritative by many organizations, including state Boards of Accountancy and the American Institute of CPAs (AICPA). The FASB is based in Norwalk, Connecticut, and is led by seven full-time Board members, one being the chairman, appointed by the Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) to serve five-year terms and are eligible for one term reappointment.\n\nThe FASB is subject to oversight by the Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF), which selects the members of the FASB and the Governmental Accounting Standards Board and funds both organizations. The Board of Trustees of the FAF is selected by a nomination process that involves several organizations from investing, accounting, business, financial, and governmental sectors, but are ultimately selected by the existing Board. The selection process was amended as such in 2008 to reduce private sector influence on the Board of Trustees and its oversight of the FASB and GASB. Marshall Armstrong, then-president of the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA), appointed a group of seven men (collectively called the Wheat Committee after its head Francis Wheat) in 1971 to examine the organization and operation of the Accounting Principles Board, in order to determine what adjustments were needed to facilitate more accurate and timely results and avoid governmental rule-making.",
        "is_citable": true,
        "prompt_context_metadata": {
          "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Financial_Accounting_Standards_Board",
          "search_provider": "anthropic",
          "age": "2 weeks ago"
        }
      },
      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "An overview of the FASB ASC (Accounting Standards Codification) - GAAP Dynamics",
        "url": "https://www.gaapdynamics.com/back-to-school-a-fasb-asc-accounting-standards-codification-how-to/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "gaapdynamics.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=gaapdynamics.com",
          "site_name": "GAAP Dynamics"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "By having consistently applied \u201caccounting rules\u201d, results and data can be compared across entities by users of the financial statements. The FAF states that \u201chigh quality financial accounting and reporting standards promote better information in the marketplace. Better information fosters greater transparency. Transparent, relevant information helps investors and lenders make better decisions about where to put their money with confidence.\u201d \u00b7 The FASB standards are easy to find (and they are free)! Simply go to the FASB\u2019s website, select \u201cStandards\u201d at the top of the site, and then \u201cAccounting Standards Codification\u201d. Or, you can select the direct link to the ASC near the top of the FASB\u2019s homepage. Once you\u2019ve found your way to the ASC, you must check that you are not a robot and then select \u201cAccess\u201d. Once you\u2019ve entered the ASC (and accepted a licensing agreement), the guidance is ready for your research!\n\nBy having consistently applied \u201caccounting rules\u201d, results and data can be compared across entities by users of the financial statements. The FAF states that \u201chigh quality financial accounting and reporting standards promote better information in the marketplace. Better information fosters greater transparency. Transparent, relevant information helps investors and lenders make better decisions about where to put their money with confidence.\u201d \u00b7 The FASB standards are easy to find (and they are free)! Simply go to the FASB\u2019s website, select \u201cStandards\u201d at the top of the site, and then \u201cAccounting Standards Codification\u201d. Or, you can select the direct link to the ASC near the top of the FASB\u2019s homepage. Once you\u2019ve found your way to the ASC, you must check that you are not a robot and then select \u201cAccess\u201d. Once you\u2019ve entered the ASC (and accepted a licensing agreement), the guidance is ready for your research!\n\nThis blog will discuss the \u201caccounting rules\u201d found in the FASB Accounting Standards Codification (ASC), such as who establishes the content, how to locate and navigate, and how new rules are created. The Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) has two standard-setting boards: the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB). These boards work together to develop U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (U.S. GAAP). State and local governments use GASB established standards, while public and private entities and non-profits use FASB established standards (we\u2019ll focus on the FASB standards in this post). The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has designated the FASB to be the standard setter for public companies and many other organizations have also recognized the FASB\u2019s standards to be authoritative as well.\n\nSome industries have unique transactions that require specific guidance, so the FASB created industry focused topics within the ASC. It\u2019s important to note that the industry specific guidance is incremental, entities must also follow guidance in other Topics that do not conflict with the industry-specific guidance. There are currently 15 industry topics in the ASC, such as Agriculture, Airlines, Entertainment, Extractive Activities, Financial Services, Software, and U.S. Steamship Entities. We frequently get asked, \u201cHow is accounting guidance issued by the SEC incorporated into the ASC?\u201d \u00b7 Since the FASB is not responsible for issuing or maintaining SEC guidance, the SEC guidance is not the same as the other guidance found in the ASC. However, as a convenience to its users, the FASB acknowledged that this information is essential for the financial reporting of public companies and, as a result, has incorporated certain guidance from the SEC into the ASC.\n\nThe accounting world is constantly changing, which means the \u201caccounting rules\u201d must change too! Typically, the FASB (Board) identifies a topic or issue via requests from users or other means. Research is conducted and conversations occur amongst the Board at public meetings regarding the issues identified and analyzed by the FASB staff. Exposure drafts are then issued to obtain public input, and, in some cases, public meetings are conducted. The responses from the public are submitted through \u201cComment Letters.\u201d The comment letters and public discussions are reviewed at a subsequent public meeting by the Board before an Accounting Standard Update (ASU) is drafted, voted upon by the Board, and issued. The ASUs issued describe what is changing and the logic behind the changes as well as the effective date of the changes. ASUs are numbered based on the year issued and the update number in a sequential order (e.g., ASU 2021-01 would signify the first ASU issued in the year 2021).",
        "is_citable": true,
        "prompt_context_metadata": {
          "url": "https://www.gaapdynamics.com/back-to-school-a-fasb-asc-accounting-standards-codification-how-to/",
          "search_provider": "anthropic",
          "age": "February 20, 2025"
        }
      }
    ],

      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Accounting Standards Codification - Wikipedia",
        "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accounting_Standards_Codification",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "wikipedia.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=wikipedia.org",
          "site_name": "Wikipedia"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "All users must register to view any codification information. The codification allows a free basic view or paid professional view to the public. The professional view requires an annual subscription up to $940 depending on concurrent users. Discounts may apply to multiple concurrent users.\n\nIn US accounting practices, the Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) is the current single source of United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). It is maintained by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB). The codification is effective for interim and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. All prior accounting standards documents were superseded as described in FASB Statement No. 168, The FASB Accounting Standards Codification and the Hierarchy of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. Accounting literature not included in the Codification is non-authoritative. The Codification reorganizes the thousands of U.S. GAAP pronouncements into roughly 90 accounting topics and displays all topics using a consistent structure. It also includes relevant U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) guidance that follows the same topical structure in separate sections in the Codification.\n\nThe Financial Accounting Standards Advisory Council then voiced its concerns due to the increase of financial reporting guidance from the old U.S. GAAP standards, and the FASB responded by launching a new project to codify the standards. The project was approved in September 2004 by the Trustees of the Financial Accounting Foundation. All users must register to view any codification information. The codification allows a free basic view or paid professional view to the public. The professional view requires an annual subscription up to $940 depending on concurrent users. Discounts may apply to multiple concurrent users. The codification is used by accounting and reporting professionals, analysts and investors. Each ASC reference is structured as a series of four numbers separated by hyphens: a three-digit Topic (the first digit of which represents an Area), a two-digit Subtopic, a two-digit Section, and a two- or three-digit Paragraph.",
        "is_citable": true,
        "prompt_context_metadata": {
          "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accounting_Standards_Codification",
          "search_provider": "anthropic",
          "age": "October 2, 2025"
        }
      },

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "It includes citations to the FASB&#x27;s Accounting Standards Codification organized alphabetically by topic. There is a section of &quot;uncertain tax positions&quot; which is one of the ACC 550 research topics. The library also owns hard copies of the Accounting Policy &amp; Practice Portfolios. They can be found in the Reference section (REF HF 5616 .U5 .A345). Each portfolio will bear a number after the call number. For example: Accounting for Income Taxes: Uncertain Tax Positions (FIN 48) (REF HF 5616.45 .A345 No. 5002) Accounting for Income Taxes: Uncertain Tax Positions - Selected Topics (REF HF 5616.45 .A345 No. 5003) More information: View a list of all portfolios. Checkpoint is the best database to use to find Codification standards. ... This library handout has search tips on how to use the database. ... You can use the RIA Checkpoint database to access the FASB pre-codification standards.\n\nRIA Checkpoint database will provide you with access to Accounting and Auditing materials, including the new FASB Accounting Standards Codification and the prior standards and codes. ... Go to the index of these reference books and look up your topic for more information. Georgiades, G. (2011). GAAP Financial statement disclosures manual. Chicago: CCH. (REF HF 5658 .M55 2018-19) This reference book is a source of financial statement disclosures and key presentation requirements. There are hundreds of examples of sample footnote disclosures. Use this reference work to look up an ASC topic and get an executive summary, the disclosure and key presentation requirements, and examples of financial statement disclosures. Topics are arranged numerically into 9 parts, consistent with the organization of the Codification. Part 1: General Principles (ASC Topics 100s) Part 2: Presentation (ASC Topics 200s) Part 3: Assets (ASC Topics 300s) Part 4: Liabilities (ASC Topics 400s) Part 5: Equity (ASC T\n\n(REF HF 5681 .B2 .A35) The annual reference work compiles annual reporting and disclosure data with examples drawn from a survey of the annual reports of some 500 publicly traded companies. ASC (Accounting Standards Codification) is included when referenced in the statements of the reporting entities. Siegel, J. G., Levine, M. H., Qureshi, A. A., & Shim, J. K. (2018). GAAP Handbook of policies and procedures. Chicago: CCH. (REF HF 5616 .U5 .S54) This practical reference book contains all of the important authoritative pronouncements on GAAP. It contains accounting principles, financial reporting presentation requirements, recommended disclosures, and specialized accounting topics. Williams, J. R., Carcello, J. V., & Neal, T. L. (2011). GAAP Guide. Chicago: CCH. (REF HF 5616 .U5 .M526 2011) 2 vols. This reference guide analyzes AICPA Accounting Research Bulletins, APB Opinions, and FASB Statements and Interpretations.\n\nIt includes citations to the FASB's Accounting Standards Codification organized alphabetically by topic. There is a section of \"uncertain tax positions\" which is one of the ACC 550 research topics. The library also owns hard copies of the Accounting Policy & Practice Portfolios. They can be found in the Reference section (REF HF 5616 .U5 .A345). Each portfolio will bear a number after the call number. For example: Accounting for Income Taxes: Uncertain Tax Positions (FIN 48) (REF HF 5616.45 .A345 No. 5002) Accounting for Income Taxes: Uncertain Tax Positions - Selected Topics (REF HF 5616.45 .A345 No. 5003) More information: View a list of all portfolios. Checkpoint is the best database to use to find Codification standards. ... This library handout has search tips on how to use the database. ... You can use the RIA Checkpoint database to access the FASB pre-codification standards.\n\nOn July 1, 2009 the Financial Accounting standards Board (FASB) instituted a major change in the way accounting standards are organized. The FASB Accounting Standards Codification (FASB Codification) became the single official source of authoritative, nongovernmental U.S. generally accepted accounting principles (U.S. GAAP). Now only one level of authoritative U.S. GAAP exists, other than guidance issued by the SEC. All other literature is non-authoritative. The Codification reorganizes thousands of U.S. GAAP pronouncements into roughly 90 accounting Topics, and displays all Topics using a consistent structure. Also included is relevant SEC guidance that follows the same topical structure used in the Codification. The reorganization is thought to simplify U.S. GAAP authoritative literature. The Codification includes the following authoritative literature issued by standard setters other than the SEC that apply to all entities (other than governmental entities): \u2022 Financial Accounting S",

        "text": "Topic 105, Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, for additional details. The Codification structure is significantly different from the structure of previous standards. This document provides information that will help in obtaining a good understanding of the \u00b7 Codification structure, ...\n\nTopic 105, Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, for additional details. The Codification structure is significantly different from the structure of previous standards. This document provides information that will help in obtaining a good understanding of the \u00b7 Codification structure, content, and style. ... System) streamlines the research process. The Codification Research System has numerous ... Derivative Instruments and Hedging Activities, is an amendment of FASB Statement No. 133, Accounting for Derivative Instruments and Hedging Activities, so the content of Statement 149 is \u00b7 included through the as-amended version of Statement 133. As of July 1, 2009, the Codification was composed of the following literature issued by various ... Commission approval. If you find any discrepancies between the SEC content as presented in the Codification and the \u00b7\n\nThe FASB developed a hybrid classification system specifically for the Codification. The following is the structure of the classification system: XXX-YY-ZZ-PP where XXX = Topic, YY = Subtopic, ZZ = Section, PP = Paragraph In \u00b7 the case of SEC content, an \u201cS\u201d precedes the Section number. ... Topics represent a collection of related guidance. For example, Leases is a Topic. The Topics \u00b7 correlate very closely to standards issued by the International Accounting Standards Board ... Topic or by general Topic. ... Subtopics represent subsets of a Topic and are generally distinguished by type or by scope. For \u00b7 example, Operating Leases and Capital Leases are two Subtopics of the Leases Topic \u00b7 distinguished by type of lease. Each Topic contains an Overall Subtopic that generally represents \u00b7 the pervasive guidance for the Topic. Each additional Subtopic represents incremental or unique \u00b7\n\nTopic. The use of a consistent code allows the FASB to create one version of the content that \u00b7 resides in a single location but allows users to access the same content from multiple locations in \u00b7 the navigation structure. For example, a user can access the system from the perspective of the \u00b7 agriculture industry looking for inventory content. Alternatively, the user could access inventory \u00b7 and find the agriculture industry content. In this case, Codification Topic 330 represents \u00b7 Inventory and Codification Topic 905 represents the Agriculture Industry. As noted above, industry content resides in the Industry Topic. Therefore, Codification Subtopic \u00b7 905-330 represents the Inventory Subtopic of the Agriculture Industry. When browsing \u00b7 Codification Topic 330, users will see Subtopic 905\u2014Agriculture. That represents an \u00b7 intersection link to the content in Codification Subtopic 905-330.\n\nCodification Topic 105 includes a discussion of materiality. Over the years, various standards \u00b7 have included language stating that a standard is applicable if an item is material. Retaining \u00b7 such individual materiality references in the Codification could lead users to believe that \u00b7 materiality applies to one set of standards, but not another. This is not the case. ... With the issuance of FASB Statement No. 168, The FASB Accounting Standards Codification\u00ae \u00b7 and the Hierarchy of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, the FASB approved the \u00b7 Codification as the source of authoritative US GAAP for nongovernmental entities for interim \u00b7 and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. The Codification supersedes all existing \u00b7 non-SEC standards. The Codification Research System will include the superseded standards for ... Standards Update Instructions.",

        "text": "... This Topic contains three subtopics (Overall, Discontinued Operations, and Liquidation Basis of Accounting). ... This Topic contains two Subtopics (Overall and Offsetting). ... This Topic has been moved to Topic 505, Equity, as part of the FASB\u2019s project on Codification improvements.\n\nASC 995 U.S. Steamship Entities ... The Codification\u2019s categories are organized by Topic, Subtopic, Section, and paragraph, each with a numerical designation. The Sections in every Subtopic are labeled uniformly, as follows: The Codification is updated via Accounting Standards Updates (ASUs). These are assigned a number that corresponds to the year of the ASU\u2019s issuance and its sequential order (e.g., the first ASU issued in 2010 was 2010-01). ASUs replace accounting changes that historically were issued as FASB Statements, FASB Interpretations, FASB Staff Positions, or other types of FASB standards. ASUs contain a background and basis for conclusions as well as a marked draft of any changes to existing guidance. The new guidance is labeled \u201cPending Content\u201d in the Codification, and the superseded guidance will not be removed until the guidance in the ASU is effective for all entities.\n\nThe FASB Accounting Standards Codification is the source of authoritative generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) recognized by the FASB to be applied to nongovernmental entities. The Codification is effective for interim and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. ... You must log in to view this content and have a subscription package that includes this content. ... This Topic establishes the FASB Accounting Standards Codification (Codification) as the source of authoritative GAAP recognized by the FASB to be applied by nongovernmental entities. ... This Topic contains three subtopics (Overall, Discontinued Operations, and Liquidation Basis of Accounting). ... This Topic contains two Subtopics (Overall and Offsetting). ... This Topic has been moved to Topic 505, Equity, as part of the FASB\u2019s project on Codification improvements.\n\nThis Topic has been moved to Topic 210, Balance Sheet, as part of the FASB\u2019s project on Codification improvements. ... This Topic provides general guidance for receivables and notes that receivables arise from credit sales, loans, or other transactions. ... This Topic provides detailed guidance on the accounting and reporting of \u201cinvestments in equity securities that have readily determinable fair values\u201d and \u201call investments in debt securities.\u201d ... This Topic provides detailed guidance for \u201cinvestments in equity securities and other ownership interests in an entity, including investments in partnerships, unincorporated joint ventures, and limited liability companies as if those other ownership interests are equity securities.\u201d ... This Topic comprises three Subtopics (Overall; Partnerships, Joint Ventures, and Limited Liability Entities; and Income Taxes).\n\nThis Topic simply provides a link to the guidance in the Codification on deferred revenue related to vendor sales incentives, which is in ASC 605-50. ... This Topic provides guidance for general commitments, such as \u201cunused letters of credit; preferred stock dividends in arrears; commitments such as those for plant acquisition; and obligations to reduce debts, maintain working capital, or restrict dividends.\u201d ... This Topic outlines the accounting and disclosure requirements for loss and gain contingencies. ... This Topic \u201cestablishes the accounting and disclosure requirements to be met by a guarantor for certain guarantees issued and outstanding.\u201d ...         "text": "The FASB Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) reorganizes accounting literature. In addition to General Principles (Topic 105), offering an explanation of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP), the Seven financial accounting and reporting categories, which are numbered, are: ... Industry Category (Topics 905 through 944) contains specific industry guidance. ... Notice to Constituents provides information on the Codification structure, style and history. ... The seven categories have topics, and the topics have subtopics. If needed, subtopics are divided into sections, which are numbered consistently across all subtopics.         "is_missing": false,
        "text": "FASB Concept Statements The FASB Concepts Statements are intended to serve the public interest by setting the objectives, qualitative characteristics, and other concepts that guide selection of economic phenomena to be recognized and measured for financial reporting and their display in financial statements or related means of communicating information to those who are interested. Concepts Statements guide the Board in developing sound accounting principles and provide the Board and its constituents with an understanding of the appropriate content and inherent limitations of financial reporting. A Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts does not establish generally accepted accounting standards. ... Useful free tool to use to keep up to date on changes to the codification. This isn&#x27;t the codification itself. It is the list of topics that have changed or been added since the codification took place in 2009.\n\na. FASB ASC Codification reference, for example: i. Topics \u2014 FASB ASC 310 to access the Receivables Topic ii. Subtopics \u2014 FASB ASC 310-10 to access the Overall Subtopic of Topic 310 iii. Sections \u2014 FASB ASC 310-10-15 to access the Scope Section of Subtopic 310-10 iv. Paragraph \u2014 FASB ASC 310-10-15-2 to access paragraph 2 of Section 310-10-15\" Note: \"For clarity, companies may wish to include the title of the cited Codification section. \"FASB ASC Topic 310\" would appear as \"FASB ASC Topic 310, Receivables and \"FASB ASC Section 310-10-15\" would appear as \"FASB ASC paragraph 310-10-15, Receivables - Overall - Scope.\" To assist readers in the transition to the Codification, companies may present both the Codification citation and former reference. If companies use this presentation, citations to the former reference should follow the Codification reference in parentheses.\n\nFASB Concept Statements The FASB Concepts Statements are intended to serve the public interest by setting the objectives, qualitative characteristics, and other concepts that guide selection of economic phenomena to be recognized and measured for financial reporting and their display in financial statements or related means of communicating information to those who are interested. Concepts Statements guide the Board in developing sound accounting principles and provide the Board and its constituents with an understanding of the appropriate content and inherent limitations of financial reporting.         "text": "The FASB Accounting Standards Codification is the source of authoritative generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) recognized by the FASB to be applied to nongovernmental entities. The Codification is effective for interim and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. ... You must log in to view this content and have a subscription package that includes this content. ... This Topic establishes the FASB Accounting Standards Codification (Codification) as the source of authoritative GAAP recognized by the FASB to be applied by nongovernmental entities. ... This Topic contains three subtopics (Overall, Discontinued Operations, and Liquidation Basis of Accounting). ... This Topic contains two Subtopics (Overall and Offsetting). ... This Topic has been moved to Topic 505, Equity, as part of the FASB\u2019s project on Codification improvements.\n\nThe Codification is updated via Accounting Standards Updates (ASUs). These are assigned a number that corresponds to the year of the ASU\u2019s issuance and its sequential order (e.g., the first ASU issued in 2010 was 2010-01). ASUs replace accounting changes that historically were issued as FASB Statements, FASB Interpretations, FASB Staff Positions, or other types of FASB standards. ASUs contain a background and basis for conclusions as well as a marked draft of any changes to existing guidance. The new guidance is labeled \u201cPending Content\u201d in the Codification, and the superseded guidance will not be removed until the guidance in the ASU is effective for all entities.\n\nThis Topic has been moved to Topic 210, Balance Sheet, as part of the FASB\u2019s project on Codification improvements. ... This Topic provides general guidance for receivables and notes that receivables arise from credit sales, loans, or other transactions. ... This Topic provides detailed guidance on the accounting and reporting of \u201cinvestments in equity securities that have readily determinable fair values\u201d and \u201call investments in debt securities.\u201d ... This Topic provides detailed guidance for \u201cinvestments in equity securities and other ownership interests in an entity, including investments in partnerships, unincorporated joint ventures, and limited liability companies as if those other ownership interests are equity securities.\u201d ... This Topic comprises three Subtopics (Overall; Partnerships, Joint Ventures, and Limited Liability Entities; and Income Taxes).\n\nThis Topic \u201cestablishes standards for how an issuer classifies and measures in its statement of financial position certain financial instruments with characteristics of both liabilities and equity.\u201d ... This Topic provides guidance on several specific elements of transactions, accounts, and financial instruments that are classified as components of equity as well as overall general guidance related to equity. ... This Topic provides guidance for transaction-specific revenue recognition and certain matters related to revenue-generating activities, such as the sale of products, the rendering of services, and the gain or loss on involuntary conversions of nonmonetary assets to monetary assets, that are not addressed specifically in other Topics. ...       {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Amendments to the FASB Accounting Standards Codification\u00ae",
        "url": "https://viewpoint.pwc.com/dt/us/en/fasb_financial_accou/asus_fulltext/2014/asu_201409revenue_fr/asu_201409revenue_fr_US/asu_201409revenue_fr_US/amendments_to_the_fa__70_US.html",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "pwc.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=pwc.com",
          "site_name": "PwC Viewpoint"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Granting licenses (see paragraphs 606-10-55-54 through 55-65) Granting options to purchase additional goods or services (when those options provide a customer with a material right, as described in paragraphs 606-10-55-41 through 55-45). 606-10-25-19 A good or service that is promised to a customer is distinct if both of the following criteria are met: The customer can benefit from the good or service either on its own or together with other resources that are readily available to the customer (that is, the good or service is capable of being distinct). The entity\u2019s promise to transfer the good or service to the customer is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract (that is, the good or service is distinct within the context of the contract). 606-10-25-20 A customer can benefit from a good or service in accordance with paragraph 606-10-25-19(a) if the good or service could be used, consumed, sold for an amount that is greater than scrap value, or otherwise held in a w\n\nAn agreement between two or more parties that creates enforceable rights and obligations. ... An entity\u2019s right to consideration in exchange for goods or services that the entity has transferred to a customer when that right is conditioned on something other than the passage of time (for example, the entity\u2019s future performance). ... An entity\u2019s obligation to transfer goods or services to a customer for which the entity has received consideration (or the amount is due) from the customer. ... A party that has contracted with an entity to obtain goods or services that are an output of the entity\u2019s ordinary activities in exchange for consideration. ... An entity that possesses the following characteristics, in varying degrees, that distinguish it from a business entity: Contributions of significant amounts of resources from resource providers who do not expect commensurate or proportionate pecuniary return \u00b7\n\nIn some industries and jurisdictions, a contract modification may be described as a change order, a variation, or an amendment. A contract modification exists when the parties to a contract approve a modification that either creates new or changes existing enforceable rights and obligations of the parties to the contract. A contract modification could be approved in writing, by oral agreement, or implied by customary business practices. If the parties to the contract have not approved a contract modification, an entity shall continue to apply the guidance in this Topic to the existing contract until the contract modification is approved. 606-10-25-11 A contract modification may exist even though the parties to the contract have a dispute about the scope or price (or both) of the modification or the parties have approved a change in the scope of the contract but have not yet determined the corresponding change in price.\n\nThe entity has transferred physical possession of the asset\u2014The customer\u2019s physical possession of an asset may indicate that the customer has the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset or to restrict the access of other entities to those benefits. However, physical possession may not coincide with control of an asset. For example, in some repurchase agreements and in some consignment arrangements, a customer or consignee may have physical possession of an asset that the entity controls. Conversely, in some bill-and-hold arrangements, the entity may have physical possession of an asset that the customer controls. Paragraphs 606-10-55-66 through 55-78, 606-10-55-79 through 55-80, and 606-10-55-81 through 55-84 provide guidance on accounting for repurchase agreements, consignment arrangements, and bill-and-hold arrangements, respectively.\n\nGranting licenses (see paragraphs 606-10-55-54 through 55-65) Granting options to purchase additional goods or services (when those options provide a customer with a material right, as described in paragraphs 606-10-55-41 through 55-45). 606-10-25-19 A good or service that is promised to a customer is distinct if both of the following criteria are met: The customer can benefit from the good or service either on its own or together with other resources that are readily available to the customer (that is, the good or service is capable of being distinct). The entity\u2019s promise to transfer the good or service to the customer is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract (that is, the good or service is distinct within the context of the contract). 606-10-25-20 A customer can benefit from a good or service in accordance with paragraph 606-10-25-19(a) if the good or service could be used, consumed, sold for an amount that is greater than scrap value, or otherwise held in a w",

      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Accounting Standards Codification - Wikipedia",
        "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accounting_Standards_Codification",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "wikipedia.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=wikipedia.org",
          "site_name": "Wikipedia"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "All users must register to view any codification information. The codification allows a free basic view or paid professional view to the public. The professional view requires an annual subscription up to $940 depending on concurrent users. Discounts may apply to multiple concurrent users.\n\nIn US accounting practices, the Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) is the current single source of United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). It is maintained by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB). The codification is effective for interim and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. All prior accounting standards documents were superseded as described in FASB Statement No. 168, The FASB Accounting Standards Codification and the Hierarchy of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. Accounting literature not included in the Codification is non-authoritative. The Codification reorganizes the thousands of U.S. GAAP pronouncements into roughly 90 accounting topics and displays all topics using a consistent structure. It also includes relevant U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) guidance that follows the same topical structure in separate sections in the Codification.\n\nThe Financial Accounting Standards Advisory Council then voiced its concerns due to the increase of financial reporting guidance from the old U.S. GAAP standards, and the FASB responded by launching a new project to codify the standards. The project was approved in September 2004 by the Trustees of the Financial Accounting Foundation. All users must register to view any codification information. The codification allows a free basic view or paid professional view to the public. The professional view requires an annual subscription up to $940 depending on concurrent users. Discounts may apply to multiple concurrent users. The codification is used by accounting and reporting professionals, analysts and investors. Each ASC reference is structured as a series of four numbers separated by hyphens: a three-digit Topic (the first digit of which represents an Area), a two-digit Subtopic, a two-digit Section, and a two- or three-digit Paragraph.\n\nTo prepare constituents for the change, the FASB provided a number of tools and training resources. The Codification did not change GAAP, but it introduced a new structure, organized in an easily accessible, user-friendly online research system. The Codification is publicly available. The FASB expected the system to reduce the amount of time and effort required to research accounting issues, mitigate the risk of noncompliance with standards through improved usability of the literature, provide accurate information with real-time updates as new standards are released, and assist the FASB with the research efforts required during the standard-setting process. The three primary goals of the codification are \"simplify user access by codifying all authoritative U.S. GAAP in one spot, ensure that the codification content accurately represented authoritative U.S.",
        "is_citable": true,
        "prompt_context_metadata": {
          "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accounting_Standards_Codification",
          "search_provider": "anthropic",
          "age": "October 2, 2025"
        }
      },
      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "FAF expands free online access to FASB, GASB accounting standards | Accounting Today",
        "url": "https://www.accountingtoday.com/news/faf-expands-free-online-access-to-fasb-gasb-accounting-standards",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "accountingtoday.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=accountingtoday.com",
          "site_name": "Accounting Today"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Free versions of both the codification and GARS have been available online for years, but the new system promises to offer enhanced features compared to the current free offering (known as &quot;Basic View&quot;). They include improvements in navigation, ...\n\nJanuary 30, 2023, 12:49 p.m. EST 1 Min Read ... The Financial Accounting Foundation, the group that oversees the Financial Accounting Standards Board and the Governmental Accounting Standards Board, plans to improve free online access to both boards' accounting standards codifications for the general public, make them easier to use and eliminate the paid professional service. ... Governmental Accounting Research System to make financial accounting standards more widely accessible to stakeholders and the public. The FAF said it hasn't yet determined the firm date for this change, but it's expected to happen this spring. Free versions of both the codification and GARS have been available online for years, but the new system promises to offer enhanced features compared to the current free offering (known as \"Basic View\"). They include improvements in navigation, search, printing, copy and paste, and the ability to offer feedback.\n\nThe Accounting Standards Codification is the complete, official version of U.S. GAAP published by FASB and used by public companies, private companies, nonprofit organizations, and employee benefit plans in the U.S. The Governmental Accounting Research System is the complete, official version of U.S. GAAP published by GASB and used by states, cities and other government entities in the U.S. FASB, GASB and FAF logos on the wall at headquarters in Norwalk, Connecticut ... PCAOB appoints new acting general counsel and acting director of the PCAOB's Division of Enforcement and Investigations; RSM admits 48 to partner; and more news from across the profession. ... Plus, Data Ramp solution lets people process data without moving it; Rillet adds quarterly and annual financial reports, other features; and additional accounting tech news. ... Experts shared the tools, trends and technologies they think will shape the next 12 months in accounting.",
        "is_citable": true,
        "prompt_context_metadata": {
          "url": "https://www.accountingtoday.com/news/faf-expands-free-online-access-to-fasb-gasb-accounting-standards",
          "search_provider": "anthropic",
          "age": "January 30, 2023"
        }
      },
      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "FASB Codification - ACCT 431: Auditing - LibGuides at Indiana University of PA",
        "url": "https://libraryguides.lib.iup.edu/c.php?g=563354&p=4347591",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "iup.edu",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=iup.edu",
          "site_name": "Iup"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Free access: All members of the public may access the Codification through the FASB Accounting Standards Codification Basic View. Users must establish individual free accounts. Search features available in the Professional View are not available in the Basic View.\n\nThe FASB Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) reorganizes accounting literature. In addition to General Principles (Topic 105), offering an explanation of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP), the eight financial accounting and reporting areas are: ... Industry (Topics 905 through 944) contains specific industry guidance. The Codification contains only accounting guidance, not auditing, tax preparation, or other kinds of guidance. The Codification contains only authoritative guidance. Nonauthoritiative guidance is not included in the Codification content itself. The Codification is applicable only to U.S. entities. It does not contain GAAP for other countries or International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS). The Codification is the source of authoritivative U.S. GAAP recognized by the FASB to be applied to nongovernmental entities.\n\nFree access: All members of the public may access the Codification through the FASB Accounting Standards Codification Basic View. Users must establish individual free accounts. Search features available in the Professional View are not available in the Basic View. ... Search functions, advanced navigation, and other features are not available with this view. Includes: ... Utility to identify the location of legacy standards. ... Cross Reference enables you to cross reference between the superceded standards and the Codification. Either: Enter the information about the earlier standard to learn where in the Codification the language is now located, or enter information about the Codification to identify the standards that contain the language referenced in the Codification. See Cross Reference help and tutorial \u00b7 Additional ways to search in Professional View or Academic Access: Enter keyword or keywords in search box (available in the Professional View-Academic Access and CCH Accountin",
        "is_citable": true,

          "url": "https://libraryguides.lib.iup.edu/c.php?g=563354&p=4347591",
          "search_provider": "anthropic"

        "title": "FAF Grants Free Online Access to FASB Codification and GARS | DART \u2013 Deloitte Accounting Research Tool",

          "site_name": "Deloitte DART"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "The Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) has officially launched its free online access to the FASB Accounting Standards Codification\u00ae and the Governmental Accounting Research System\u2122 (GARS); accordingly, the previous paid \u201cProfessional View\u201d subscription tier is being eliminated.\n\nThe Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) has officially launched its free online access to the FASB Accounting Standards Codification\u00ae and the Governmental Accounting Research System\u2122 (GARS); accordingly, the previous paid \u201cProfessional View\u201d subscription tier is being eliminated. The FAF has indicated that its aim in granting free access to the Codification and GARS is to enhance the accessibility of accounting standards for both stakeholders and the wider public.",

        "text": "The Financial Accounting Foundation, ... Board, plans to improve free online access to both boards&#x27; accounting standards codifications for the general public, make them easier to use and eliminate the paid professional service...\n\nJanuary 30, 2023, 12:49 p.m. EST 1 Min Read ... The Financial Accounting Foundation, the group that oversees the Financial Accounting Standards Board and the Governmental Accounting Standards Board, plans to improve free online access to both boards' accounting standards codifications for the general public, make them easier to use and eliminate the paid professional service. ... Governmental Accounting Research System to make financial accounting standards more widely accessible to stakeholders and the public. The FAF said it hasn't yet determined the firm date for this change, but it's expected to happen this spring. Free versions of both the codification and GARS have been available online for years, but the new system promises to offer enhanced features compared to the current free offering (known as \"Basic View\"). They include improvements in navigation, search, printing, copy and paste, and the ability to offer feedback.\n\nAs a result, the \"Professional View\" paid subscription service will be eliminated and users who previously accessed Professional View can instead use the enhanced free versions. Prorated refunds will be issued for subscribers whose paid terms extend beyond the cutover date. \"We believe this move, which is consistent with a recent recommendation from the Investor Advisory Committee of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, will increase our stakeholders' access to these important resources, and thereby improve the understanding and implementation of financial accounting standards in the United States,\" said FAF executive director John Auchincloss in a statement. The SEC's Investor Advisory Committee submitted a set of recommendations last year to the SEC calling for broad changes at FASB to make it more responsive to the needs of investors ( ...       {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "FAF debuts enhanced access to online accounting standards - Journal of Accountancy",
        "url": "https://www.journalofaccountancy.com/news/2023/feb/faf-debuts-enhanced-access-online-accounting-standards/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "journalofaccountancy.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=journalofaccountancy.com",
          "site_name": "Journal of Accountancy"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "These include enhancements to navigation, search, printing, and copy/paste. With the change, the former \u201cProfessional View\u201d paid subscription service has been eliminated, according to FAF.\n\nThe Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) on Monday released its free, enhanced online access to the Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) and the Governmental Accounting Research System (GARS), which was announced to stakeholders last month. The Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) said on Monday it is planning to give investors and other stakeholders greater online access to financial accounting standards for public and private companies, as well as for governments. The FAF, which oversees both the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB), will be providing free, online access to the Accounting Standards Codification and the Governmental Accounting Research System starting sometime this spring. The Accounting Standards Codification is the complete and official version of Generally Accepted Accounting Standards (GAAP) published by the FASB and used by public companies, private companies, nonprofit organizations, and employee benefit plans in the U.S. The Governmental Accounting Research System (GARS) is the complete and official version of GAAP published by the GASB and used by states, cities, and other governmental entities in the U.S.\n\nAs a result of this move, the \u201cProfessional View\u201d paid subscription service will be eliminated and users who previously accessed Professional View can instead use the enhanced free versions of the codification and GARS, according to FAF. Current Professional View subscribers will be transitioned off the current system, and prorated refunds will be issued for those subscribers whose paid terms extend beyond the cutover date. \u201cWe believe this move, which is consistent with a recent recommendation from the Investor Advisory Committee of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, will increase our stakeholders\u2019 access to these important resources, and thereby improve the understanding and implementation of financial accounting standards in the United States,\u201d FAF Executive Director John Auchincloss said in a written statement. The FAF has not yet set a firm date for this change to online access to the accounting standards, but it is expected to occur this spring. ... Jason Bramwell Jan.",

### ðŸ¤– Assistant  *01:50:50 UTC*

OK here's the full picture. It's not a mafia situation â€” it's actually the opposite. The door IS open, it's just that the history makes it *look* like there's a secret handshake.

**The timeline that creates the confusion:**

Before February 2023, the FASB Codification had two tiers: a crippled "Basic View" (free, but no search, no copy/paste, limited navigation) and a "Professional View" ($940/year per user). PwC, Deloitte, KPMG, Thomson Reuters Checkpoint, Wolters Kluwer CCH â€” they all had **licensing agreements** with the Financial Accounting Foundation to reproduce the full codification text within their paid platforms. That's the commercial arrangement: FAF licenses the text, firms pay to embed it in their research tools alongside their own interpretive guides.

**Then the SEC's Investor Advisory Committee told FAF to stop gatekeeping.** In January 2023, FAF announced it was eliminating the paid tier entirely, and in February 2023 they launched full free access at **asc.fasb.org**. The Professional View is gone. Everyone gets everything â€” full search, copy/paste, printing, the whole codification, every paragraph of every topic.

**What PwC Viewpoint actually is:**

Two layers, and the trick is understanding which is which:

1. **Free tier (no account needed):** All PwC guides (the 30+ accounting and reporting guides we identified before), all news, all "In depth" publications, AND â€” here's the key â€” **all US GAAP and US GASB content is free.** That includes the FASB Codification embedded in their platform. Their own pricing flyer says it explicitly: *"US GAAP and US GASB related content is available free of charge by registering for a free Viewpoint account."*

2. **Paid tier ($$$):** PwC's proprietary Accounting and Reporting Manual (ARM), the SEC Volume, IFRS content (which requires a separate license from the IFRS Foundation), and international content. This is PwC's *own* work product â€” their internal guidance manuals that their audit teams use.

**The actual access paths you have RIGHT NOW:**

**Path 1: asc.fasb.org (the source)**
- Full FASB Codification, all ~90 topics, every paragraph
- Free registration, full search, copy/paste enabled
- No API, no bulk download â€” it's a web application
- But you CAN manually navigate topic by topic and copy text
- The terms say "personal non-commercial use" unless you get written permission â€” but this is the same copyright gray area we discussed (private body writing what functions as law)

**Path 2: Every single ASU (Accounting Standards Update) is a free PDF on fasb.org**
- These are the *amendments* to the Codification
- They contain the **full verbatim text** of every paragraph being added/changed/deleted
- They're at storage.fasb.org â€” direct PDF links
- Example: ASU 2014-09 (Revenue Recognition/ASC 606) contains the entire Topic 606 text
- You can reconstruct major portions of the current Codification by collecting all the ASUs

**Path 3: PwC Viewpoint free tier**
- Register free at viewpoint.pwc.com
- Full ASU text is hosted on their platform (I found verbatim ASC 606 paragraphs there)
- All their accounting guides contain labeled ASC excerpts with paragraph citations
- Their guides state this explicitly: *"excerpts from the FASB's Accounting Standards Codification... clearly labelled"*

**Path 4: Deloitte DART**
- The paid tier includes a full Codification browser (their FASB ASC Codification Manual)
- But the free Roadmap guides contain extensive ASC quotes
- Every ASU analysis on dart.deloitte.com includes the amendment text

**So what's the actual play for building a GAAP knowledge base?**

You don't need to scrape anything or find a secret door. The strategy is layered:

1. **Primary source:** Go to asc.fasb.org, register free, and systematically read/copy the topics relevant to your knowledge base. For a tax advisory practice, you probably need maybe 15-20 topics max (revenue recognition, income taxes, leases, financial instruments, etc.)

2. **Supplementary:** Download the key ASU PDFs from storage.fasb.org â€” these are unrestricted PDFs on a public server. They contain full paragraph text with tracked changes showing exactly what was added/removed.

3. **Interpretation layer:** PwC's free guides on Viewpoint give you the Big 4's read on how each standard actually works in practice, with direct ASC citations. These are arguably *more* useful than the raw codification because they include the Q&As and examples that practitioners actually need.

4. **The XBRL Taxonomy** (the one I found earlier with the open license) â€” this gives you the complete structural map of every element in the Codification. It's machine-readable, explicitly allows reproduction in "Permitted Works that comment on, explain, or assist in the use or implementation" â€” and includes every paragraph reference.

The "mafia" perception comes from the *old* regime (pre-2023) when FAF was charging $940/year and licensing to the Big 4. The SEC essentially forced them to open it up. The Big 4 still have licensing agreements for embedding the text in their commercial platforms, but you don't need their platforms to get the text â€” you can get it from the source for free.

ASC 985-20 establishes the accounting and reporting for the costs of software to be sold, leased, or \u00b7 otherwise marketed as a separate product or as part of a product or process (externally marketed \u00b7 software), whether internally developed or purchased. ASC 985-20 applies to development costs for \u00b7 software that will be separately licensed to customers (e.g., on-premises software), as well as software \u00b7 included within a product sold to a customer. For example, ASC 985-20 applies to software embedded \u00b7 in a semiconductor chip that is used as a component of a product that is sold to a customer (e.g., automobile electronic systems).         "text": "The FASB Accounting Standards Codification \u00ae is the source of authoritative generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) recognized by the FASB to be applied by nongovernmental entities. An Accounting Standards Update is not authoritative; rather, it is a document that communicates how the Accounting Standards Codification is being amended. It also provides other information to help a user of GAAP understand how and why GAAP is changing and when the changes will be effective. For additional copies of this Accounting Standards Update and information on applicable prices and discount rates contact: Order Department Financial Accounting Standards Board 401 Merritt 7 PO Box 5116 Norwalk, CT 06856-5116 \u00b7 Please ask for our Product Code No. ASU2020-03. FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING SERIES (ISSN 0885-9051) is published monthly with the exception of May, July, and October by the Financial Accounting Foundation, 401 Merritt 7, PO Box 5116, Norwalk, CT 06856-5116.\n\nThe amendments to the FASB Accounting Standards Codification \u00ae in this Accounting Standards Update require improvements to the U.S. GAAP Financial Reporting Taxonomy (Taxonomy). Those improvements, which will be incorporated into the proposed 2021 Taxonomy, are available through ... Please ensure that you select Print Background (colors and images) when printing. ... Welcome to Viewpoint, the new platform that replaces Inform.\n\nThe FASB Accounting Standards Codification \u00ae is the source of authoritative generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) recognized by the FASB to be applied by nongovernmental entities. An Accounting Standards Update is not authoritative; rather, it is a document that communicates how the Accounting Standards Codification is being amended. It also provides other information to help a user of GAAP understand how and why GAAP is changing and when the changes will be effective. For additional copies of this Accounting Standards Update and information on applicable prices and discount rates contact: Order Department Financial Accounting Standards Board 401 Merritt 7 PO Box 5116 Norwalk, CT 06856-5116 \u00b7 Please ask for our Product Code No. ASU2020-03. FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING SERIES (ISSN 0885-9051) is published monthly with the exception of May, July, and October by the Financial Accounting Foundation, 401 Merritt 7, PO Box 5116, Norwalk, CT 06856-5116.\n\nWhy Is the FASB Issuing This Accounting Standards Update (Update)? The Board has an ongoing project on its agenda for improving the Codification or correcting its unintended application. The items addressed in that project generally are not expected to have a significant effect on current accounting practice or create a significant administrative cost for most entities. The amendments in this Update are similar to those items. However, the Board decided to issue a separate Update to improve various financial instruments Topics in the Codification to increase stakeholder awareness of the amendments and to expedite the improvement process. An explanation of why each amendment in this Update is being made is provided in the \u201cAmendments to the FASB Accounting Standards Codification\u00ae\u201d section. Thus, there is no separate section for the Board\u2019s basis for conclusions in this Update.",

    "thinking": "Now I'm getting somewhere. Let me piece this together:\n\n1. Since February 2023, FAF eliminated the paid \"Professional View\" and made the FULL FASB ASC available free at asc.fasb.org\n2. PwC Viewpoint has TWO tiers:\n   - FREE tier: All PwC guides, news, AND all US GAAP/GASB content (including FASB Codification)\n   - PAID tier: PwC's proprietary manuals (ARM - Accounting & Reporting Manual), SEC volume, and IFRS/international content\n3. PwC's guides literally contain \"excerpts from the FASB's Accounting Standards Codification\" - clearly labeled, with PwC interpretation alongside\n\n\n4. So there's no secret door here\u2014the entire FASB Codification is already public and free. PwC just has a license to reproduce excerpts in their guides, same as anyone else could. The real story is that FAF actually moved away from the \"mafia\" model by making it free in 2023 after pressure from the SEC's Investor Advisory Committee, whereas they used to charge $940/year for Professional View.\n\nNow I should look into how other commercial databases like RIA Checkpoint fit into this picture.",
    "summaries": [
      {
        "summary": "Unraveling PwC's access pathways to accounting standards."
      },
      {
        "summary": "Debunked collusion theory; revealed public access and licensing arrangements."
      }
    ],
    "cut_off": false,
    "alternative_display_type": null
  },

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Now let me check the actual terms at asc.fasb.org and what commercial research platforms offer:",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "The improvements in the 2024 GRT resulted from a review of reporting requirements \u00b7 for various ASC Topics and assigning references with a disclosureRef role to elements \u00b7 intended to meet those requirements.         "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "FASB Issues ASU to Remove Concepts Statement References From Codification (April 24, 2024)",
        "url": "https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/publications/deloitte/heads-up/2024/fasb-issues-asu-to-remove-concepts-statement-references-from-codification",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "deloitte.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=deloitte.com",
          "site_name": "Deloitte DART"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "This Heads Up discusses FASB Accounting Standards Update No. 2024-02, Codification Improvements \u2014 Amendments to Remove References to the Concepts Statements (ASC).\n\nAn entity that adopts the amendments in an interim period would have to adopt them as of the beginning of the fiscal year that includes that interim period. Early adoption is permitted for all entities in any period in which financial statements have not yet been issued or available for issuance. An entity would choose whether to apply the amendments (1) prospectively to new transactions after the adoption date or (2) retrospectively to the beginning of the earliest comparative period presented. ... FASB Accounting Standards Update (ASU) No. 2024-02, Codification Improvements \u2014 Amendments to Remove References to the Concepts Statements. ... FASB Concepts Statement No. 7, Using Cash Flow Information and Present Value in Accounting Measurements. ... For titles of ASC references, see Deloitte\u2019s \u201cTitles of Topics and Subtopics in the FASB Accounting Standards Codification.\u201d ...\n\nOn March 29, 2024, the FASB issued ASU 2024-02,1 which removes references to the Board\u2019s concepts statements from the FASB Accounting Standards Codification (the \u201cCodification\u201d or ASC). The ASU is part of the Board\u2019s standing project to make \u201cCodification updates for technical corrections such as conforming amendments, clarifications to guidance, simplifications to wording or the structure of guidance, and other minor improvements.\u201d \u00b7 Concepts statements are nonauthoritative and do not establish generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP). As noted on the FASB\u2019s Web site, they guide the Board in \u201cdeveloping sound accounting principles and provide the Board and its constituents with an understanding of the appropriate content and inherent limitations of financial reporting.\u201d Before establishing the Codification in 2009, the FASB used or referred to the concepts statements as part of its standard setting.",

        "title": "Codification | DART \u2013 Deloitte Accounting Research Tool",
        "url": "https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/codification",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "deloitte.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=deloitte.com",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "ASUs are available in PDF format on the FASB\u2019s Web site.\n\nASC 995 U.S. Steamship Entities ... The Codification\u2019s categories are organized by Topic, Subtopic, Section, and paragraph, each with a numerical designation. The Sections in every Subtopic are labeled uniformly, as follows: The Codification is updated via Accounting Standards Updates (ASUs). These are assigned a number that corresponds to the year of the ASU\u2019s issuance and its sequential order (e.g., the first ASU issued in 2010 was 2010-01). ASUs replace accounting changes that historically were issued as FASB Statements, FASB Interpretations, FASB Staff Positions, or other types of FASB standards. ASUs contain a background and basis for conclusions as well as a marked draft of any changes to existing guidance. The new guidance is labeled \u201cPending Content\u201d in the Codification, and the superseded guidance will not be removed until the guidance in the ASU is effective for all entities.\n\nThe FASB Accounting Standards Codification is the source of authoritative generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) recognized by the FASB to be applied to nongovernmental entities. The Codification is effective for interim and annual periods ending after September 15, 2009. ... You must log in to view this content and have a subscription package that includes this content. ... This Topic establishes the FASB Accounting Standards Codification (Codification) as the source of authoritative GAAP recognized by the FASB to be applied by nongovernmental entities. ... This Topic contains three subtopics (Overall, Discontinued Operations, and Liquidation Basis of Accounting). ... This Topic contains two Subtopics (Overall and Offsetting). ... This Topic has been moved to Topic 505, Equity, as part of the FASB\u2019s project on Codification improvements.\n\nThis Topic has been moved to Topic 210, Balance Sheet, as part of the FASB\u2019s project on Codification improvements. ... This Topic provides general guidance for receivables and notes that receivables arise from credit sales, loans, or other transactions. ... This Topic provides detailed guidance on the accounting and reporting of \u201cinvestments in equity securities that have readily determinable fair values\u201d and \u201call investments in debt securities.\u201d ... This Topic provides detailed guidance for \u201cinvestments in equity securities and other ownership interests in an entity, including investments in partnerships, unincorporated joint ventures, and limited liability companies as if those other ownership interests are equity securities.\u201d ... This Topic comprises three Subtopics (Overall; Partnerships, Joint Ventures, and Limited Liability Entities; and Income Taxes).\n\nASUs are available in PDF format on the FASB\u2019s Web site.",

        "title": "FASB & GARS | American Accounting Association",
        "url": "https://aaahq.org/Research/FASB-GARS",

          "site_name": "American Accounting Association"

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "The Accounting Standards Codification\u00ae (\u201cthe Codification\u201d) is the complete and official version of Generally Accepted Accounting Standards (GAAP) published by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and used by public companies, private companies, nonprofit organizations, and employee benefit plans in the United States. The Governmental Accounting Research System\u2122 (\u201cGARS\u201d) is the complete and official version of GAAP published by the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB) and used by states, cities, and other governmental entities in the United States. While free versions of both the Codification and GARS have been available online for years, the new system provides enhanced features compared to the former free offering. These include enhancements to navigation, search, printing, and copy/paste. As a result of this change, the former \u201cProfessional View\u201d paid subscription service has been eliminated. The URLs to access the updated websites are: Accounting Standards Codi\n\nThe Accounting Standards Codification\u00ae (\u201cthe Codification\u201d) is the complete and official version of Generally Accepted Accounting Standards (GAAP) published by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and used by public companies, private companies, nonprofit organizations, and employee benefit plans in the United States. The Governmental Accounting Research System\u2122 (\u201cGARS\u201d) is the complete and official version of GAAP published by the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB) and used by states, cities, and other governmental entities in the United States. While free versions of both the Codification and GARS have been available online for years, the new system provides enhanced features compared to the former free offering. These include enhancements to navigation, search, printing, and copy/paste. As a result of this change, the former \u201cProfessional View\u201d paid subscription service has been eliminated. What if I want to use the PCS to find the effective FASB Codification content for a past or ... A. You may use a past or future date; however, the system limits the \u201cFiscal Year Beginning \u00b7 Date\u201d field to a three-year window before and after the actual date the user is utilizing in the \u00b7 PCS. The PCS is not intended for finding the effective FASB Codification content for an \u00b7 extended past or future period. An issue in backdating is that certain standards that would have \u00b7 been pending at the backdated time may have already become current and are no longer available \u00b7 as pending content. In addition, current content at that historical point in time is only available in \u00b7 the Archive (not through the PCS tool).
