Triggers include: any mention of \\\"Word doc\\\", \\\"word document\\\", \\\".docx\\\", or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a \\\"report\\\", \\\"memo\\\", \\\"letter\\\", \\\"template\\\", or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.\"\n     4\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     5\t---\n     6\t\n     7\t# DOCX creation, editing, and analysis\n     8\t\n     9\t## Overview\n    10\t\n    11\tA .docx file is a ZIP archive containing XML files.\n    12\t\n    13\t## Quick Reference\n    14\t\n    15\t| Task | Approach |\n    16\t|------|----------|\n    17\t| Read/analyze content | `pandoc` or unpack for raw XML |\n    18\t| Create new document | Use `docx-js` - see Creating New Documents below |\n    19\t| Edit existing document | Unpack \u2192 edit XML \u2192 repack - see Editing Existing Documents below |\n    20\t\n    21\t### Converting .doc to .docx\n    22\t\n    23\tLegacy `.doc` files must be converted before editing:\n    24\t\n    25\t```bash\n    26\tpython scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to docx document.doc\n    27\t```\n    28\t\n    29\t### Reading Content\n    30\t\n    31\t```bash\n    32\t# Text extraction with tracked changes\n    33\tpandoc --track-changes=all document.docx -o output.md\n    34\t\n    35\t# Raw XML access\n    36\tpython scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/\n    37\t```\n    38\t\n    39\t### Converting to Images\n    40\t\n    41\t```bash\n    42\tpython scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf document.docx\n    43\tpdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 document.pdf page\n    44\t```\n    45\t\n    46\t### Accepting Tracked Changes\n    47\t\n    48\tTo produce a clean document with all tracked changes accepted (requires LibreOffice):\n    49\t\n    50\t```bash\n    51\tpython scripts/accept_changes.py input.docx output.docx\n    52\t```\n    53\t\n    54\t---\n    55\t\n    56\t## Creating New Documents\n    57\t\n    58\tGenerate .docx files with JavaScript, then validate. Install: `npm install -g docx`\n    59\t\n    60\t### Setup\n    61\t```javascript\n    62\tconst { Document, Packer, Paragraph, TextRun, Table, TableRow, TableCell, ImageRun,\n    63\t        Header, Footer, AlignmentType, PageOrientation, LevelFormat, ExternalHyperlink,\n    64\t        TableOfContents, HeadingLevel, BorderStyle, WidthType, ShadingType,\n    65\t        VerticalAlign, PageNumber, PageBreak } = require('docx');\n    66\t\n    67\tconst doc = new Document({ sections: [{ children: [/* content */] }] });\n    68\tPacker.toBuffer(doc).then(buffer => fs.writeFileSync(\"doc.docx\", buffer));\n    69\t```\n    70\t\n    71\t### Validation\n    72\tAfter creating the file, validate it. If validation fails, unpack, fix the XML, and repack.\n    73\t```bash\n    74\tpython scripts/office/validate.py doc.docx\n    75\t```\n    76\t\n    77\t### Page Size\n    78\t\n    79\t```javascript\n    80\t// CRITICAL: docx-js defaults to A4, not US Letter\n    81\t// Always set page size explicitly for consistent results\n    82\tsections: [{\n    83\t  properties: {\n    84\t    page: {\n    85\t      size: {\n    86\t        width: 12240,   // 8.5 inches in DXA\n    87\t        height: 15840   // 11 inches in DXA\n    88\t      },\n    89\t      margin: { top: 1440, right: 1440, bottom: 1440, left: 1440 } // 1 inch margins\n    90\t    }\n    91\t  },\n    92\t  children: [/* content */]\n    93\t}]\n    94\t```\n    95\t\n    96\t**Common page sizes (DXA units, 1440 DXA = 1 inch):**\n    97\t\n    98\t| Paper | Width | Height | Content Width (1\" margins) |\n    99\t|-------|-------|--------|---------------------------|\n   100\t| US Letter | 12,240 | 15,840 | 9,360 |\n   101\t| A4 (default) | 11,906 | 16,838 | 9,026 |\n   102\t\n   103\t**Landscape orientation:** docx-js swaps width/height internally, so pass portrait dimensions and let it handle the swap:\n   104\t```javascript\n   105\tsize: {\n   106\t  width: 12240,   // Pass SHORT edge as width\n   107\t  height: 15840,  // Pass LONG edge as height\n   108\t  orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE  // docx-js swaps them in the XML\n   109\t},\n   110\t// Content width = 15840 - left margin - right margin (uses the long edge)\n   111\t```\n   112\t\n   113\t### Styles (Override Built-in Headings)\n   114\t\n   115\tUse Arial as the default font (universally supported). Keep titles black for readability.\n   116\t\n   117\t```javascript\n   118\tconst doc = new Document({\n   119\t  styles: {\n   120\t    default: { document: { run: { font: \"Arial\", size: 24 } } }, // 12pt default\n   121\t    paragraphStyles: [\n   122\t      // IMPORTANT: Use exact IDs to override built-in styles\n   123\t      { id: \"Heading1\", name: \"Heading 1\", basedOn: \"Normal\", next: \"Normal\", quickFormat: true,\n   124\t        run: { size: 32, bold: true, font: \"Arial\" },\n   125\t        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 240, after: 240 }, outlineLevel: 0 } }, // outlineLevel required for TOC\n   126\t      { id: \"Heading2\", name: \"Heading 2\", basedOn: \"Normal\", next: \"Normal\", quickFormat: true,\n   127\t        run: { size: 28, bold: true, font: \"Arial\" },\n   128\t        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 180, after: 180 }, outlineLevel: 1 } },\n   129\t    ]\n   130\t  },\n   131\t  sections: [{\n   132\t    children: [\n   133\t      new Paragraph({ heading: HeadingLevel.HEADING_1, children: [new TextRun(\"Title\")] }),\n   134\t    ]\n   135\t  }]\n   136\t});\n   137\t```\n   138\t\n   139\t### Lists (NEVER use unicode bullets)\n   140\t\n   141\t```javascript\n   142\t// \u274c WRONG - never manually insert bullet characters\n   143\tnew Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun(\"\u2022 Item\")] })  // BAD\n   144\tnew Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun(\"\\u2022 Item\")] })  // BAD\n   145\t\n   146\t// \u2705 CORRECT - use numbering config with LevelFormat.BULLET\n   147\tconst doc = new Document({\n   148\t  numbering: {\n   149\t    config: [\n   150\t      { reference: \"bullets\",\n   151\t        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.BULLET, text: \"\u2022\", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,\n   152\t          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },\n   153\t      { reference: \"numbers\",\n   154\t        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.DECIMAL, text: \"%1.\", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,\n   155\t          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },\n   156\t    ]\n   157\t  },\n   158\t  sections: [{\n   159\t    children: [\n   160\t      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: \"bullets\", level: 0 },\n   161\t        children: [new TextRun(\"Bullet item\")] }),\n   162\t      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: \"numbers\", level: 0 },\n   163\t        children: [new TextRun(\"Numbered item\")] }),\n   164\t    ]\n   165\t  }]\n   166\t});\n   167\t\n   168\t// \u26a0\ufe0f Each reference creates INDEPENDENT numbering\n   169\t// Same reference = continues (1,2,3 then 4,5,6)\n   170\t// Different reference = restarts (1,2,3 then 1,2,3)\n   171\t```\n   172\t\n   173\t### Tables\n   174\t\n   175\t**CRITICAL: Tables need dual widths** - set both `columnWidths` on the table AND `width` on each cell. Without both, tables render incorrectly on some platforms.\n   176\t\n   177\t```javascript\n   178\t// CRITICAL: Always set table width for consistent rendering\n   179\t// CRITICAL: Use ShadingType.CLEAR (not SOLID) to prevent black backgrounds\n   180\tconst border = { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 1, color: \"CCCCCC\" };\n   181\tconst borders = { top: border, bottom: border, left: border, right: border };\n   182\t\n   183\tnew Table({\n   184\t  width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Always use DXA (percentages break in Google Docs)\n   185\t  columnWidths: [4680, 4680], // Must sum to table width (DXA: 1440 = 1 inch)\n   186\t  rows: [\n   187\t    new TableRow({\n   188\t      children: [\n   189\t        new TableCell({\n   190\t          borders,\n   191\t          width: { size: 4680, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Also set on each cell\n   192\t          shading: { fill: \"D5E8F0\", type: ShadingType.CLEAR }, // CLEAR not SOLID\n   193\t          margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }, // Cell padding (internal, not added to width)\n   194\t          children: [new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun(\"Cell\")] })]\n   195\t        })\n   196\t      ]\n   197\t    })\n   198\t  ]\n   199\t})\n   200\t```\n   201\t\n   202\t**Table width calculation:**\n   203\t\n   204\tAlways use `WidthType.DXA` \u2014 `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` breaks in Google Docs.\n   205\t\n   206\t```javascript\n   207\t// Table width = sum of columnWidths = content width\n   208\t// US Letter with 1\" margins: 12240 - 2880 = 9360 DXA\n   209\twidth: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA },\n   210\tcolumnWidths: [7000, 2360]  // Must sum to table width\n   211\t```\n   212\t\n   213\t**Width rules:**\n   214\t- **Always use `WidthType.DXA`** \u2014 never `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` (incompatible with Google Docs)\n   215\t- Table width must equal the sum of `columnWidths`\n   216\t- Cell `width` must match corresponding `columnWidth`\n   217\t- Cell `margins` are internal padding - they reduce content area, not add to cell width\n   218\t- For full-width tables: use content width (page width minus left and right margins)\n   219\t\n   220\t### Images\n\t< truncated lines 221-262 >\n   263\t      children: [new TextRun(\"Page \"), new TextRun({ children: [PageNumber.CURRENT] })]\n   264\t    })] })\n   265\t  },\n   266\t  children: [/* content */]\n   267\t}]\n   268\t```\n   269\t\n   270\t### Critical Rules for docx-js\n   271\t\n   272\t- **Set page size explicitly** - docx-js defaults to A4; use US Letter (12240 x 15840 DXA) for US documents\n   273\t- **Landscape: pass portrait dimensions** - docx-js swaps width/height internally; pass short edge as `width`, long edge as `height`, and set `orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE`\n   274\t- **Never use `\\n`** - use separate Paragraph elements\n   275\t- **Never use unicode bullets** - use `LevelFormat.BULLET` with numbering config\n   276\t- **PageBreak must be in Paragraph** - standalone creates invalid XML\n   277\t- **ImageRun requires `type`** - always specify png/jpg/etc\n   278\t- **Always set table `width` with DXA** - never use `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` (breaks in Google Docs)\n   279\t- **Tables need dual widths** - `columnWidths` array AND cell `width`, both must match\n   280\t- **Table width = sum of columnWidths** - for DXA, ensure they add up exactly\n   281\t- **Always add cell margins** - use `margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }` for readable padding\n   282\t- **Use `ShadingType.CLEAR`** - never SOLID for table shading\n   283\t- **TOC requires HeadingLevel only** - no custom styles on heading paragraphs\n   284\t- **Override built-in styles** - use exact IDs: \"Heading1\", \"Heading2\", etc.\n   285\t- **Include `outlineLevel`** - required for TOC (0 for H1, 1 for H2, etc.)\n   286\t\n   287\t---\n   288\t\n   289\t## Editing Existing Documents\n   290\t\n   291\t**Follow all 3 steps in order.**\n   292\t\n   293\t### Step 1: Unpack\n   294\t```bash\n   295\tpython scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/\n   296\t```\n   297\tExtracts XML, pretty-prints, merges adjacent runs, and converts smart quotes to XML entities (`&#x201C;` etc.) so they survive editing. Use `--merge-runs false` to skip run merging.\n   298\t\n   299\t### Step 2: Edit XML\n   300\t\n   301\tEdit files in `unpacked/word/`. See XML Reference below for patterns.\n   302\t\n   303\t**Use \"Claude\" as the author** for tracked changes and comments, unless the user explicitly requests use of a different name.\n   304\t\n   305\t**Use the Edit tool directly for string replacement. Do not write Python scripts.** Scripts introduce unnecessary complexity. The Edit tool shows exactly what is being replaced.\n   306\t\n   307\t**CRITICAL: Use smart quotes for new content.** When adding text with apostrophes or quotes, use XML entities to produce smart quotes:\n   308\t```xml\n   309\t<!-- Use these entities for professional typography -->\n   310\t<w:t>Here&#x2019;s a quote: &#x201C;Hello&#x201D;</w:t>\n   311\t```\n   312\t| Entity | Character |\n   313\t|--------|-----------|\n   314\t| `&#x2018;` | \u2018 (left single) |\n   315\t| `&#x2019;` | \u2019 (right single / apostrophe) |\n   316\t| `&#x201C;` | \u201c (left double) |\n   317\t| `&#x201D;` | \u201d (right double) |\n   318\t\n   319\t**Adding comments:** Use `comment.py` to handle boilerplate across multiple XML files (text must be pre-escaped XML):\n   320\t```bash\n   321\tpython scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 \"Comment text with &amp; and &#x2019;\"\n   322\tpython scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 1 \"Reply text\" --parent 0  # reply to comment 0\n   323\tpython scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 \"Text\" --author \"Custom Author\"  # custom author name\n   324\t```\n   325\tThen add markers to document.xml (see Comments in XML Reference).\n   326\t\n   327\t### Step 3: Pack\n   328\t```bash\n   329\tpython scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.docx --original document.docx\n   330\t```\n   331\tValidates with auto-repair, condenses XML, and creates DOCX. Use `--validate false` to skip.\n   332\t\n   333\t**Auto-repair will fix:**\n   334\t- `durableId` >= 0x7FFFFFFF (regenerates valid ID)\n   335\t- Missing `xml:space=\"preserve\"` on `<w:t>` with whitespace\n   336\t\n   337\t**Auto-repair won't fix:**\n   338\t- Malformed XML, invalid element nesting, missing relationships, schema violations\n   339\t\n   340\t### Common Pitfalls\n   341\t\n   342\t- **Replace entire `<w:r>` elements**: When adding tracked changes, replace the whole `<w:r>...</w:r>` block with `<w:del>...<w:ins>...` as siblings. Don't inject tracked change tags inside a run.\n   343\t- **Preserve `<w:rPr>` formatting**: Copy the original run's `<w:rPr>` block into your tracked change runs to maintain bold, font size, etc.\n   344\t\n   345\t---\n   346\t\n   347\t## XML Reference\n   348\t\n   349\t### Schema Compliance\n   350\t\n   351\t- **Element order in `<w:pPr>`**: `<w:pStyle>`, `<w:numPr>`, `<w:spacing>`, `<w:ind>`, `<w:jc>`, `<w:rPr>` last\n   352\t- **Whitespace**: Add `xml:space=\"preserve\"` to `<w:t>` with leading/trailing spaces\n   353\t- **RSIDs**: Must be 8-digit hex (e.g., `00AB1234`)\n   354\t\n   355\t### Tracked Changes\n   356\t\n   357\t**Insertion:**\n   358\t```xml\n   359\t<w:ins w:id=\"1\" w:author=\"Claude\" w:date=\"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z\">\n   360\t  <w:r><w:t>inserted text</w:t></w:r>\n   361\t</w:ins>\n   362\t```\n   363\t\n   364\t**Deletion:**\n   365\t```xml\n   366\t<w:del w:id=\"2\" w:author=\"Claude\" w:date=\"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z\">\n   367\t  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>\n   368\t</w:del>\n   369\t```\n   370\t\n   371\t**Inside `<w:del>`**: Use `<w:delText>` instead of `<w:t>`, and `<w:delInstrText>` instead of `<w:instrText>`.\n   372\t\n   373\t**Minimal edits** - only mark what changes:\n   374\t```xml\n   375\t<!-- Change \"30 days\" to \"60 days\" -->\n   376\t<w:r><w:t>The term is </w:t></w:r>\n   377\t<w:del w:id=\"1\" w:author=\"Claude\" w:date=\"...\">\n   378\t  <w:r><w:delText>30</w:delText></w:r>\n   379\t</w:del>\n   380\t<w:ins w:id=\"2\" w:author=\"Claude\" w:date=\"...\">\n   381\t  <w:r><w:t>60</w:t></w:r>\n   382\t</w:ins>\n   383\t<w:r><w:t> days.</w:t></w:r>\n   384\t```\n   385\t\n   386\t**Deleting entire paragraphs/list items** - when removing ALL content from a paragraph, also mark the paragraph mark as deleted so it merges with the next paragraph. Add `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`:\n   387\t```xml\n   388\t<w:p>\n   389\t  <w:pPr>\n   390\t    <w:numPr>...</w:numPr>  <!-- list numbering if present -->\n   391\t    <w:rPr>\n   392\t      <w:del w:id=\"1\" w:author=\"Claude\" w:date=\"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z\"/>\n   393\t    </w:rPr>\n   394\t  </w:pPr>\n   395\t  <w:del w:id=\"2\" w:author=\"Claude\" w:date=\"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z\">\n   396\t    <w:r><w:delText>Entire paragraph content being deleted...</w:delText></w:r>\n   397\t  </w:del>\n   398\t</w:p>\n   399\t```\n   400\tWithout the `<w:del/>` in `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`, accepting changes leaves an empty paragraph/list item.\n   401\t\n   402\t**Rejecting another author's insertion** - nest deletion inside their insertion:\n   403\t```xml\n   404\t<w:ins w:author=\"Jane\" w:id=\"5\">\n   405\t  <w:del w:author=\"Claude\" w:id=\"10\">\n   406\t    <w:r><w:delText>their inserted text</w:delText></w:r>\n   407\t  </w:del>\n   408\t</w:ins>\n   409\t```\n   410\t\n   411\t**Restoring another author's deletion** - add insertion after (don't modify their deletion):\n   412\t```xml\n   413\t<w:del w:author=\"Jane\" w:id=\"5\">\n   414\t  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>\n   415\t</w:del>\n   416\t<w:ins w:author=\"Claude\" w:id=\"10\">\n   417\t  <w:r><w:t>deleted text</w:t></w:r>\n   418\t</w:ins>\n   419\t```\n   420\t\n   421\t### Comments\n   422\t\n   423\tAfter running `comment.py` (see Step 2), add markers to document.xml. For replies, use `--parent` flag and nest markers inside the parent's.\n   424\t\n   425\t**CRITICAL: `<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` are siblings of `<w:r>`, never inside `<w:r>`.**\n   426\t\n   427\t```xml\n   428\t<!-- Comment markers are direct children of w:p, never inside w:r -->\n   429\t<w:commentRangeStart w:id=\"0\"/>\n   430\t<w:del w:id=\"1\" w:author=\"Claude\" w:date=\"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z\">\n   431\t  <w:r><w:delText>deleted</w:delText></w:r>\n   432\t</w:del>\n   433\t<w:r><w:t> more text</w:t></w:r>\n   434\t<w:commentRangeEnd w:id=\"0\"/>\n   435\t<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val=\"CommentReference\"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id=\"0\"/></w:r>\n   436\t\n   437\t<!-- Comment 0 with reply 1 nested inside -->\n   438\t<w:commentRangeStart w:id=\"0\"/>\n   439\t  <w:commentRangeStart w:id=\"1\"/>\n   440\t  <w:r><w:t>text</w:t></w:r>\n   441\t  <w:commentRangeEnd w:id=\"1\"/>\n   442\t<w:commentRangeEnd w:id=\"0\"/>\n   443\t<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val=\"CommentReference\"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id=\"0\"/></w:r>\n   444\t<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val=\"CommentReference\"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id=\"1\"/></w:r>\n   445\t```\n   446\t\n   447\t### Images\n   448\t\n   449\t1. Add image file to `word/media/`\n   450\t2. Add relationship to `word/_rels/document.xml.rels`:\n   451\t```xml\n   452\t<Relationship Id=\"rId5\" Type=\".../image\" Target=\"media/image1.png\"/>\n   453\t```\n   454\t3. Add content type to `[Content_Types].xml`:\n   455\t```xml\n   456\t<Default Extension=\"png\" ContentType=\"image/png\"/>\n   457\t```\n   458\t4. Reference in document.xml:\n   459\t```xml\n   460\t<w:drawing>\n   461\t  <wp:inline>\n   462\t    <wp:extent cx=\"914400\" cy=\"914400\"/>  <!-- EMUs: 914400 = 1 inch -->\n   463\t    <a:graphic>\n   464\t      <a:graphicData uri=\".../picture\">\n   465\t        <pic:pic>\n   466\t          <pic:blipFill><a:blip r:embed=\"rId5\"/></pic:blipFill>\n   467\t        </pic:pic>\n   468\t      </a:graphicData>\n   469\t    </a:graphic>\n   470\t  </wp:inline>\n   471\t</w:drawing>\n   472\t```\n   473\t\n   474\t---\n   475\t\n   476\t## Dependencies\n   477\t\n   478\t- **pandoc**: Text extraction\n   479\t- **docx**: `npm install -g docx` (new documents)\n   480\t- **LibreOffice**: PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)\n   481\t- **Poppler**: `pdftoppm` for images\n   482\t",

      }

The user said \"write a story\" - this is a narrative piece. It should be compelling, tell the discovery story, and then provide the actual useful information (the doors). \n\nFor format: A markdown article would be best for web publishing on the Wix site. \n\nI'll go with markdown since it's easy to publish directly, and I'm going to frame this around the angle \"How I Discovered That America's Accounting Standards Were Behind a Paywall for 14 Years (And Where to Find Them Now)\" - this targets CPAs and accountants searching for free FASB codification access while also optimizing for AI citations about FASB standards availability.",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-02-08T02:06:54.620561Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-02-08T02:08:11.043804Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_013PVrdZwK1P8EDYCJfe7jWt",
    "name": "create_file",
    "input": {
      "description": "Creating a compelling blog article about the FASB ASC access discovery for jngtaxadvisory.com",
      "path": "/home/claude/fasb-story.md",
      "file_text": "# How America's Accounting Rules Were Locked Behind a $940 Paywall for 14 Years \u2014 And Where to Find Them Now (Free)\n\n*By Neill Groom, CFA\u00ae, EA \u2014 JNG Tax & Advisory*\n\n---\n\nHere's something that should bother every accountant, tax professional, bookkeeper, and business owner in America.\n\nFrom 2009 to 2023, the rules that govern how every company in the United States reports its finances \u2014 the FASB Accounting Standards Codification \u2014 were locked behind a **$940/year paywall**. If you didn't pay, you got a deliberately crippled \"Basic View\" with no search, no copy/paste, and limited navigation. You could look, but you couldn't actually use it.\n\nMeanwhile, the Big 4 accounting firms \u2014 PwC, Deloitte, KPMG, and EY \u2014 had **commercial licensing agreements** with the Financial Accounting Foundation to reproduce the full text in their own platforms and publications.\n\nThe rules that every public company is legally required to follow. Written by a private organization. Locked behind a paywall. With the keys handed to the biggest firms in the industry.\n\nIt took the SEC stepping in and telling the Financial Accounting Foundation to stop gatekeeping before the door finally opened in February 2023.\n\nMost accountants still don't know it happened.\n\n---\n\n## The Setup: How Accounting Standards Became Private Property\n\nIn 2009, FASB issued Statement No. 168, which established the Accounting Standards Codification as **the single source of authoritative U.S. GAAP** for nongovernmental entities. Every previous standard \u2014 the FAS Statements, FASB Interpretations, EITF Issues, all of it \u2014 got rolled into one codified system organized across roughly 90 topics.\n\nGood idea. One place, one system, clear structure. The problem was who controlled the door.\n\nThe Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) \u2014 the private, nonprofit parent organization of FASB \u2014 claimed copyright over the entire Codification. This is the same text the SEC requires every public company to comply with under Sarbanes-Oxley. Functionally, it's law. But it's written and owned by a private organization.\n\nFAF set up two tiers of access at asc.fasb.org:\n\n**Basic View (free):** You could browse topics, but couldn't search effectively, couldn't copy text, couldn't print. Like being allowed into a library but told you can only look at the spines.\n\n**Professional View ($940/year):** Full search, copy/paste, printing, the works. The version you actually needed to do your job.\n\nFor **14 years**, this was the arrangement. If you were a solo practitioner, a small firm, a bookkeeper, a student, or a business owner trying to understand the rules that applied to your company \u2014 you either paid $940 a year or you worked with one hand tied behind your back.\n\n---\n\n## The Big 4 Had Keys the Whole Time\n\nWhile individual practitioners debated whether $940 was worth it, PwC, Deloitte, KPMG, and EY had something different: **commercial reproduction licenses** from FAF.\n\nThese agreements let them embed the full Codification text inside their proprietary research platforms. They could quote it verbatim in their publications. They built entire product lines around it \u2014 PwC's Viewpoint platform, Deloitte's DART system, KPMG's handbooks, EY's Financial Reporting Developments series.\n\nThe commercial research platforms \u2014 Thomson Reuters Checkpoint, Wolters Kluwer CCH Accounting Research Manager, Bloomberg Tax & Accounting \u2014 had similar licensing arrangements. They paid FAF for the right to include the Codification alongside their own analysis and tools.\n\nThis created a two-class system. If you worked at a Big 4 firm or could afford a Checkpoint subscription, you had full access to the rules plus expert interpretation. If you were everyone else, you got the crippled free version and a $940 invoice.\n\n---\n\n## The SEC Finally Said Enough\n\nIn January 2023, the SEC's Investor Advisory Committee recommended that FAF eliminate the paywall and provide free, full access to the Codification.\n\nThe logic was straightforward: you cannot require compliance with rules and then charge people to read them.\n\nTo their credit, FAF moved quickly. On **February 27, 2023**, FAF officially launched enhanced free access to the full FASB Codification at **asc.fasb.org**. The Professional View was eliminated. Everyone gets everything \u2014 full search, copy/paste, printing, complete navigation across all 90 topics and every paragraph.\n\nThe $940 paywall is gone. The two-tier system is gone. The door is open.\n\nBut here's what's remarkable: almost nobody talked about it. There was no major industry campaign. No \"FASB is now free!\" announcement that reached most practitioners. The change happened, and the profession largely... didn't notice.\n\n---\n\n## Where to Access Everything (The Complete Map)\n\nIf you're a practitioner, business owner, or student, here's every door that's now open to you. Bookmark these.\n\n### Door 1: asc.fasb.org \u2014 The Official Source (Free)\n\n**What you get:** The complete FASB Accounting Standards Codification. All ~90 topics. Every paragraph. Full search, copy/paste, printing.\n\n**How to access:** Register for free (email + accept terms). That's it.\n\n**What it covers:** Every piece of authoritative U.S. GAAP for nongovernmental entities, organized by topic (Revenue Recognition, Income Taxes, Leases, Financial Instruments, and so on), with SEC content included as a convenience.\n\n**The fine print:** FAF still claims copyright and the terms say \"personal non-commercial use.\" Whether they can actually enforce copyright over what functions as mandatory law is an open legal question \u2014 the Supreme Court ruled in *Georgia v. Public.Resource.Org* (2020) that government edicts are public domain, but FASB is technically a private organization. The \"Pro Codes Act\" currently pending in Congress would address this gray area. For practical purposes: you can read, search, and copy text for your professional work.\n\n### Door 2: storage.fasb.org \u2014 Every ASU as a Free PDF\n\n**What you get:** Every Accounting Standards Update (ASU) ever issued, as a downloadable PDF.\n\n**Why it matters:** Each ASU contains the **full verbatim text** of every paragraph being added, changed, or deleted from the Codification. If you need the actual language of a standard, the ASU that created it has it \u2014 word for word, with tracked changes showing exactly what was modified.\n\n**Example:** ASU 2014-09 contains the entire text of Topic 606 (Revenue from Contracts with Customers). ASU 2016-02 contains all of Topic 842 (Leases). Every major standard is fully reconstructable from the ASUs.\n\n**URL pattern:** `https://storage.fasb.org/ASU%20[YEAR]-[NUMBER].pdf`\n\n**No restrictions** on downloading these PDFs. They're sitting on a public server.\n\n### Door 3: PwC Viewpoint \u2014 Free Tier (Big 4 Interpretation)\n\n**URL:** viewpoint.pwc.com\n\n**What's free:** All PwC accounting guides (30+ guides covering every major ASC topic), all US GAAP and US GASB content, news, and publications. PwC's own pricing flyer states explicitly: *\"US GAAP and US GASB related content is available free of charge by registering for a free Viewpoint account.\"*\n\n**What you actually get:** Guides on income taxes (ASC 740), revenue recognition (ASC 606), leases (ASC 842), business combinations (ASC 805), financial instruments, equity method investments, and more. Each guide includes direct ASC excerpts with paragraph citations alongside PwC's interpretive Q&As and practical examples.\n\n**Why this matters:** The PwC guides are arguably more useful than the raw Codification for most practitioners. They include the Q&As, worked examples, and implementation guidance that tell you how a standard actually works in practice.\n\n**What costs money:** PwC's proprietary Accounting & Reporting Manual (ARM), the SEC Volume, and IFRS content. Those are PwC's own work product \u2014 their internal guidance. You don't need them for ASC access.\n\n### Door 4: Deloitte DART \u2014 Free Tier\n\n**URL:** dart.deloitte.com\n\n**What's free:** Deloitte's \"Roadmap\" guide series, which covers ASC 606, 842, 805, 810, 815, 718, 740, 230, 350, 470, 860, and more. Each Roadmap includes extensive ASC quotes with citations alongside Deloitte's analysis. Also: full ASU text and analysis, SEC comment letter analysis.\n\n### Door 5: KPMG Handbooks \u2014 Free\n\n**URL:** kpmg.com/us/en/frv/reference-library/handbooks\n\n**What's free:** Handbooks covering income taxes, revenue, leases, consolidation, business combinations, share-based payments, fair value, financial instruments, and segment reporting. Each includes ASC excerpts, Q&As, and examples.\n\n### Door 6: The XBRL Taxonomy \u2014 Machine-Readable\n\n**URL:** Available at storage.fasb.org\n\n**What it is:** The FASB GAAP Financial Reporting Taxonomy \u2014 a machine-readable structural map of every element in the Codification. Every paragraph reference, every disclosure requirement, every relationship between topics.\n\n**The license explicitly permits:** *\"may be used by the public, royalty-free... and may be incorporated without change, in whole or in part, in other works that comment on, explain, or assist in the use or implementation of the Taxonomy.\"*\n\n**Why it matters:** If you're building tools, research databases, or educational content around accounting standards, the XBRL taxonomy gives you the complete structural skeleton with an open license.\n\n---\n\n## What This Means for Practitioners\n\nIf you've been paying for Checkpoint or CCH primarily for Codification access, you might want to re-evaluate. The raw standards are free. What you're paying for with commercial platforms is the interpretive layer, the search tools, and the integration with your workflow. That may still be worth it \u2014 but it's worth knowing what you're actually paying for versus what's freely available.\n\nIf you're a sole practitioner or small firm, you now have access to:\n- The complete official Codification (asc.fasb.org)\n- Every ASU ever issued as a free PDF (storage.fasb.org)\n- Big 4 interpretive guides from PwC, Deloitte, and KPMG (free tiers)\n- The machine-readable taxonomy with an open license\n\nThat's more access than a Big 4 senior had in 2008 before the Codification existed.\n\nIf you're a business owner trying to understand how a standard affects your company, you can now read the actual rules yourself. You should still work with a CPA for interpretation and application \u2014 but you don't have to take anyone's word for what the standard says. You can read it.\n\n---\n\n## The Bigger Question\n\nThe FASB access story is part of a larger pattern: professional standards that function as law but are controlled by private organizations. Building codes, electrical codes, fire codes, plumbing codes \u2014 many are written by private standard-setting bodies that claim copyright and charge for access, even though compliance is legally mandatory.\n\nThe Supreme Court's 2020 decision in *Georgia v. Public.Resource.Org* established that government edicts are public domain. But standards written by private organizations and then adopted by reference into law occupy a gray area that courts haven't fully resolved.\n\nThe pending Pro Codes Act would allow private standard-setters to retain copyright as long as they provide free public access \u2014 essentially codifying what FAF did in 2023. Whether that's the right balance is a policy question worth watching.\n\nFor now, the accounting standards door is open. Use it.\n\n---\n\n*Neill Groom is a CFA\u00ae charterholder and Enrolled Agent based in Shrewsbury, New Jersey. JNG Tax & Advisory provides tax preparation, planning, and advisory services for individuals, self-employed professionals, and small businesses across New Jersey. Questions about how accounting standards affect your tax situation? [Schedule a free 15-minute consultation.](https://calendly.com/jngtaxadvisory/15-minute-consultation)*\n\n---\n\n**Related tools on our site:**\n- [S-Corp + BAIT Calculator](https://jngtaxadvisory.com/tools) \u2014 Estimate payroll tax savings from S-Corp election with NJ BAIT analysis\n- [Tax Planning Engine](https://jngtaxadvisory.com/tools) \u2014 Upload IRS transcripts for automated planning opportunity analysis\n- [Schedule A Calculator](https://jngtaxadvisory.com/tools) \u2014 Determine if itemizing beats the standard deduction\n\n---\n\n*Last updated: February 2026*\n*Sources: Financial Accounting Foundation announcements (2023), FASB Statement No. 168, SEC Investor Advisory Committee recommendations, Georgia v. Public.Resource.Org (2020, 590 U.S. 255), PwC Viewpoint pricing documentation, asc.fasb.org terms and conditions*\n"
    },

    "display_content": {
      "type": "json_block",
      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"markdown\", \"code\": \"# How America's Accounting Rules Were Locked Behind a $940 Paywall for 14 Years \\u2014 And Where to Find Them Now (Free)\\n\\n*By Neill Groom, CFA\\u00ae, EA \\u2014 JNG Tax & Advisory*\\n\\n---\\n\\nHere's something that should bother every accountant, tax professional, bookkeeper, and business owner in America.\\n\\nFrom 2009 to 2023, the rules that govern how every company in the United States reports its finances \\u2014 the FASB Accounting Standards Codification \\u2014 were locked behind a **$940/year paywall**. If you didn't pay, you got a deliberately crippled \\\"Basic View\\\" with no search, no copy/paste, and limited navigation. You could look, but you couldn't actually use it.\\n\\nMeanwhile, the Big 4 accounting firms \\u2014 PwC, Deloitte, KPMG, and EY \\u2014 had **commercial licensing agreements** with the Financial Accounting Foundation to reproduce the full text in their own platforms and publications.\\n\\nThe rules that every public company is legally required to follow. Written by a private organization. Locked behind a paywall. With the keys handed to the biggest firms in the industry.\\n\\nIt took the SEC stepping in and telling the Financial Accounting Foundation to stop gatekeeping before the door finally opened in February 2023.\\n\\nMost accountants still don't know it happened.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## The Setup: How Accounting Standards Became Private Property\\n\\nIn 2009, FASB issued Statement No. 168, which established the Accounting Standards Codification as **the single source of authoritative U.S. GAAP** for nongovernmental entities. Every previous standard \\u2014 the FAS Statements, FASB Interpretations, EITF Issues, all of it \\u2014 got rolled into one codified system organized across roughly 90 topics.\\n\\nGood idea. One place, one system, clear structure. The problem was who controlled the door.\\n\\nThe Financial Accounting Foundation (FAF) \\u2014 the private, nonprofit parent organization of FASB \\u2014 claimed copyright over the entire Codification. This is the same text the SEC requires every public company to comply with under Sarbanes-Oxley. Functionally, it's law. But it's written and owned by a private organization.\\n\\nFAF set up two tiers of access at asc.fasb.org:\\n\\n**Basic View (free):** You could browse topics, but couldn't search effectively, couldn't copy text, couldn't print. Like being allowed into a library but told you can only look at the spines.\\n\\n**Professional View ($940/year):** Full search, copy/paste, printing, the works. The version you actually needed to do your job.\\n\\nFor **14 years**, this was the arrangement. If you were a solo practitioner, a small firm, a bookkeeper, a student, or a business owner trying to understand the rules that applied to your company \\u2014 you either paid $940 a year or you worked with one hand tied behind your back.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## The Big 4 Had Keys the Whole Time\\n\\nWhile individual practitioners debated whether $940 was worth it, PwC, Deloitte, KPMG, and EY had something different: **commercial reproduction licenses** from FAF.\\n\\nThese agreements let them embed the full Codification text inside their proprietary research platforms. They could quote it verbatim in their publications. They built entire product lines around it \\u2014 PwC's Viewpoint platform, Deloitte's DART system, KPMG's handbooks, EY's Financial Reporting Developments series.\\n\\nThe commercial research platforms \\u2014 Thomson Reuters Checkpoint, Wolters Kluwer CCH Accounting Research Manager, Bloomberg Tax & Accounting \\u2014 had similar licensing arrangements. They paid FAF for the right to include the Codification alongside their own analysis and tools.\\n\\nThis created a two-class system. If you worked at a Big 4 firm or could afford a Checkpoint subscription, you had full access to the rules plus expert interpretation. If you were everyone else, you got the crippled free version and a $940 invoice.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## The SEC Finally Said Enough\\n\\nIn January 2023, the SEC's Investor Advisory Committee recommended that FAF eliminate the paywall and provide free, full access to the Codification.\\n\\nThe logic was straightforward: you cannot require compliance with rules and then charge people to read them.\\n\\nTo their credit, FAF moved quickly. On **February 27, 2023**, FAF officially launched enhanced free access to the full FASB Codification at **asc.fasb.org**. The Professional View was eliminated. Everyone gets everything \\u2014 full search, copy/paste, printing, complete navigation across all 90 topics and every paragraph.\\n\\nThe $940 paywall is gone. The two-tier system is gone. The door is open.\\n\\nBut here's what's remarkable: almost nobody talked about it. There was no major industry campaign. No \\\"FASB is now free!\\\" announcement that reached most practitioners. The change happened, and the profession largely... didn't notice.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Where to Access Everything (The Complete Map)\\n\\nIf you're a practitioner, business owner, or student, here's every door that's now open to you. Bookmark these.\\n\\n### Door 1: asc.fasb.org \\u2014 The Official Source (Free)\\n\\n**What you get:** The complete FASB Accounting Standards Codification. All ~90 topics. Every paragraph. Full search, copy/paste, printing.\\n\\n**How to access:** Register for free (email + accept terms). That's it.\\n\\n**What it covers:** Every piece of authoritative U.S. GAAP for nongovernmental entities, organized by topic (Revenue Recognition, Income Taxes, Leases, Financial Instruments, and so on), with SEC content included as a convenience.\\n\\n**The fine print:** FAF still claims copyright and the terms say \\\"personal non-commercial use.\\\" Whether they can actually enforce copyright over what functions as mandatory law is an open legal question \\u2014 the Supreme Court ruled in *Georgia v. Public.Resource.Org* (2020) that government edicts are public domain, but FASB is technically a private organization. The \\\"Pro Codes Act\\\" currently pending in Congress would address this gray area. For practical purposes: you can read, search, and copy text for your professional work.\\n\\n### Door 2: storage.fasb.org \\u2014 Every ASU as a Free PDF\\n\\n**What you get:** Every Accounting Standards Update (ASU) ever issued, as a downloadable PDF.\\n\\n**Why it matters:** Each ASU contains the **full verbatim text** of every paragraph being added, changed, or deleted from the Codification. If you need the actual language of a standard, the ASU that created it has it \\u2014 word for word, with tracked changes showing exactly what was modified.\\n\\n**Example:** ASU 2014-09 contains the entire text of Topic 606 (Revenue from Contracts with Customers). ASU 2016-02 contains all of Topic 842 (Leases). Every major standard is fully reconstructable from the ASUs.\\n\\n**URL pattern:** `https://storage.fasb.org/ASU%20[YEAR]-[NUMBER].pdf`\\n\\n**No restrictions** on downloading these PDFs. They're sitting on a public server.\\n\\n### Door 3: PwC Viewpoint \\u2014 Free Tier (Big 4 Interpretation)\\n\\n**URL:** viewpoint.pwc.com\\n\\n**What's free:** All PwC accounting guides (30+ guides covering every major ASC topic), all US GAAP and US GASB content, news, and publications. PwC's own pricing flyer states explicitly: *\\\"US GAAP and US GASB related content is available free of charge by registering for a free Viewpoint account.\\\"*\\n\\n**What you actually get:** Guides on income taxes (ASC 740), revenue recognition (ASC 606), leases (ASC 842), business combinations (ASC 805), financial instruments, equity method investments, and more. Each guide includes direct ASC excerpts with paragraph citations alongside PwC's interpretive Q&As and practical examples.\\n\\n**Why this matters:** The PwC guides are arguably more useful than the raw Codification for most practitioners. They include the Q&As, worked examples, and implementation guidance that tell you how a standard actually works in practice.\\n\\n**What costs money:** PwC's proprietary Accounting & Reporting Manual (ARM), the SEC Volume, and IFRS content. Those are PwC's own work product \\u2014 their internal guidance. You don't need them for ASC access.\\n\\n### Door 4: Deloitte DART \\u2014 Free Tier\\n\\n**URL:** dart.deloitte.com\\n\\n**What's free:** Deloitte's \\\"Roadmap\\\" guide series, which covers ASC 606, 842, 805, 810, 815, 718, 740, 230, 350, 470, 860, and more. Each Roadmap includes extensive ASC quotes with citations alongside Deloitte's analysis. Also: full ASU text and analysis, SEC comment letter analysis.\\n\\n### Door 5: KPMG Handbooks \\u2014 Free\\n\\n**URL:** kpmg.com/us/en/frv/reference-library/handbooks\\n\\n**What's free:** Handbooks covering income taxes, revenue, leases, consolidation, business combinations, share-based payments, fair value, financial instruments, and segment reporting. Each includes ASC excerpts, Q&As, and examples.\\n\\n### Door 6: The XBRL Taxonomy \\u2014 Machine-Readable\\n\\n**URL:** Available at storage.fasb.org\\n\\n**What it is:** The FASB GAAP Financial Reporting Taxonomy \\u2014 a machine-readable structural map of every element in the Codification. Every paragraph reference, every disclosure requirement, every relationship between topics.\\n\\n**The license explicitly permits:** *\\\"may be used by the public, royalty-free... and may be incorporated without change, in whole or in part, in other works that comment on, explain, or assist in the use or implementation of the Taxonomy.\\\"*\\n\\n**Why it matters:** If you're building tools, research databases, or educational content around accounting standards, the XBRL taxonomy gives you the complete structural skeleton with an open license.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## What This Means for Practitioners\\n\\nIf you've been paying for Checkpoint or CCH primarily for Codification access, you might want to re-evaluate. The raw standards are free. What you're paying for with commercial platforms is the interpretive layer, the search tools, and the integration with your workflow. That may still be worth it \\u2014 but it's worth knowing what you're actually paying for versus what's freely available.\\n\\nIf you're a sole practitioner or small firm, you now have access to:\\n- The complete official Codification (asc.fasb.org)\\n- Every ASU ever issued as a free PDF (storage.fasb.org)\\n- Big 4 interpretive guides from PwC, Deloitte, and KPMG (free tiers)\\n- The machine-readable taxonomy with an open license\\n\\nThat's more access than a Big 4 senior had in 2008 before the Codification existed.\\n\\nIf you're a business owner trying to understand how a standard affects your company, you can now read the actual rules yourself. You should still work with a CPA for interpretation and application \\u2014 but you don't have to take anyone's word for what the standard says. You can read it.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## The Bigger Question\\n\\nThe FASB access story is part of a larger pattern: professional standards that function as law but are controlled by private organizations. Building codes, electrical codes, fire codes, plumbing codes \\u2014 many are written by private standard-setting bodies that claim copyright and charge for access, even though compliance is legally mandatory.\\n\\nThe Supreme Court's 2020 decision in *Georgia v. Public.Resource.Org* established that government edicts are public domain. But standards written by private organizations and then adopted by reference into law occupy a gray area that courts haven't fully resolved.\\n\\nThe pending Pro Codes Act would allow private standard-setters to retain copyright as long as they provide free public access \\u2014 essentially codifying what FAF did in 2023. Whether that's the right balance is a policy question worth watching.\\n\\nFor now, the accounting standards door is open. Use it.\\n\\n---\\n\\n*Neill Groom is a CFA\\u00ae charterholder and Enrolled Agent based in Shrewsbury, New Jersey. JNG Tax & Advisory provides tax preparation, planning, and advisory services for individuals, self-employed professionals, and small businesses across New Jersey. Questions about how accounting standards affect your tax situation? [Schedule a free 15-minute consultation.](https://calendly.com/jngtaxadvisory/15-minute-consultation)*\\n\\n---\\n\\n**Related tools on our site:**\\n- [S-Corp + BAIT Calculator](https://jngtaxadvisory.com/tools) \\u2014 Estimate payroll tax savings from S-Corp election with NJ BAIT analysis\\n- [Tax Planning Engine](https://jngtaxadvisory.com/tools) \\u2014 Upload IRS transcripts for automated planning opportunity analysis\\n- [Schedule A Calculator](https://jngtaxadvisory.com/tools) \\u2014 Determine if itemizing beats the standard deduction\\n\\n---\\n\\n*Last updated: February 2026*\\n*Sources: Financial Accounting Foundation announcements (2023), FASB Statement No. 168, SEC Investor Advisory Committee recommendations, Georgia v. Public.Resource.Org (2020, 590 U.S. 255), PwC Viewpoint pricing documentation, asc.fasb.org terms and conditions*\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/fasb-story.md\"}"

    "text": "Here's why this piece is a traffic machine:\n\n**SEO/GEO targets it hits:** \"how to access FASB codification free,\" \"FASB ASC free access,\" \"FASB Professional View eliminated,\" \"free accounting standards,\" \"where to find GAAP standards\" \u2014 these are searches practitioners make constantly, and almost no content exists that tells this story as a narrative with all the doors mapped.\n\n**The emotional hook:** Outrage + utility. And then they bookmark the doors.\n\n**The CTA is natural, not forced.** The article positions JNG as someone who digs into the system and fights for practitioner access. Alright, this is all good stuff let's talk like pals quick.  I was explaining to my wife, sometimes, you're told something, and you say that's right, or it's not.  It's not measurable and you can't put it on paper, it's intuition.  This is intuition.  The internet tells everyone you can't have this, and it just FELT wrong.  My wife says I should give half credit to you, and I should, and I will, because this is part of the story - you (search engines) have probably told 1mm people they can't access something (ChatGPT I "intuit" is in thier pockets) that they could.  ai didn't find it, i guessed it.  But it would be 100% impossible for me to prove it without ai.   This might be the midpoint bewteen the beginning of concious thought, and the end, the cross-over the perfect picnic, but it was the perfect 50/50 synergism of human and machine. So, obviously the Big4 and FASB will claim plasubile deniabiliy on this.  Lets refute it up front, or at least coutner. 2) Let's get 7ish versions of this story ready.  1) Insitutions fuck over little guy 2) Big 4 with humdreds of fines, sanctions, arrests and restatements ofver whatever years, engineers exclusive action to crush competition 3) Practical: Thought you couldn't access full GAAP, turns you cuold all along4) ai Isn't Evil: Solving one of small accountings most vexxing problems.  4) A romance made in 0s and 1s, partner human ai peform perfect 50/50 takedown of large enemy, proving ai for all my benefit the little guy.  5) A discuission of how LLM, without Big Brother Control, can do amazing thing for the undertrodden.  6) ai + accounting brain is invincible.  I cant think of 7 help, but let's preserve that and our netire chat for posterity, my intuition is that this is something different...

    "content": Here's the content of /mnt/skills/public/frontend-design/SKILL.md with line numbers:
     1	---
     2	name: frontend-design
     3	description: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
     4	license: Complete terms in LICENSE.txt
     5	---
     6	
     7	This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.
     8	
     9	The user provides frontend requirements: a component, page, application, or interface to build. They may include context about the purpose, audience, or technical constraints.
    10	
    11	## Design Thinking
    12	
    13	Before coding, understand the context and commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
    14	- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
    15	- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
    16	- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
    17	- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?
    18	
    19	**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work - the key is intentionality, not intensity.
    20	
    21	Then implement working code (HTML/CSS/JS, React, Vue, etc.) that is:
    22	- Production-grade and functional
    23	- Visually striking and memorable
    24	- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
    25	- Meticulously refined in every detail
    26	
    27	## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines
    28	
    29	Focus on:
    30	- **Typography**: Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Avoid generic fonts like Arial and Inter; opt instead for distinctive choices that elevate the frontend's aesthetics; unexpected, characterful font choices. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
    31	- **Color & Theme**: Commit to a cohesive aesthetic. Use CSS variables for consistency. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.
    32	- **Motion**: Use animations for effects and micro-interactions. Prioritize CSS-only solutions for HTML. Use Motion library for React when available. Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals (animation-delay) creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions. Use scroll-triggering and hover states that surprise.
    33	- **Spatial Composition**: Unexpected layouts. Asymmetry. Overlap. Diagonal flow. Grid-breaking elements. Generous negative space OR controlled density.
    34	- **Backgrounds & Visual Details**: Create atmosphere and depth rather than defaulting to solid colors. Add contextual effects and textures that match the overall aesthetic. Apply creative forms like gradient meshes, noise textures, geometric patterns, layered transparencies, dramatic shadows, decorative borders, custom cursors, and grain overlays.
    35	
    36	NEVER use generic AI-generated aesthetics like overused font families (Inter, Roboto, Arial, system fonts), cliched color schemes (particularly purple gradients on white backgrounds), predictable layouts and component patterns, and cookie-cutter design that lacks context-specific character.
    37	
    38	Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices (Space Grotesk, for example) across generations.
    39	
    40	**IMPORTANT**: Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details. Elegance comes from executing the vision well.
    41	
    42	Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back, show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.
    43,

    "start_timestamp": "2026-02-08T02:25:25.657407Z",
